For the city established in 2016, see [Cherbourg-en-Cotentin](/wiki/Cherbourg-en-Cotentin "Cherbourg-en-Cotentin"). For other uses, see [Cherbourg (disambiguation)](/wiki/Cherbourg_(disambiguation) "Cherbourg (disambiguation)").

**Cherbourg**[[a]](#cite_note-5) is a former [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") and [subprefecture](/wiki/Subprefectures_in_France "Subprefectures in France") located at the northern end of the [Cotentin peninsula](/wiki/Cotentin_peninsula "Cotentin peninsula") in the northwestern French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Manche](/wiki/Manche "Manche"). It was merged into the commune of [Cherbourg-Octeville](/wiki/Cherbourg-Octeville "Cherbourg-Octeville") on 28 February 2000,[[5]](#cite_note-decret-6) which was merged into the new commune of [Cherbourg-en-Cotentin](/wiki/Cherbourg-en-Cotentin "Cherbourg-en-Cotentin") on 1 January 2016.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Cherbourg is protected by [Cherbourg Harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour"), between [La Hague](/wiki/La_Hague "La Hague") and [Val de Saire](/wiki/Val_de_Saire "Val de Saire"), and the city has been a strategic position over the centuries, disputed between the English and French. Cited as one of the "keys to the kingdom" by [Vauban](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Le_Prestre_de_Vauban "Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban"), it became, by colossal maritime development work, a first-rate military port under the leadership of [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), and holds an [arsenal](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base") of the [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy"). A stopping point for prestigious transatlantic liners in the first half of the 20th century, Cherbourg was the [primary goal](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg "Battle of Cherbourg") of US troops during the [invasion of Normandy](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") in 1944.

Along with its use as a military, fishing and yachting port, it is also a [cross-Channel](/wiki/English_Channel#History_of_Channel_crossings "English Channel") ferry port, with routes to the English ports of [Poole](/wiki/Poole "Poole") and [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), and to [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") and [Rosslare Harbour](/wiki/Rosslare_Harbour "Rosslare Harbour") in Ireland. Limited by its geographical isolation from being a great commercial port, it is nonetheless an important shipbuilding centre, and a working-class city with a rural hinterland.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Cotentin_Peninsula.jpg/250px-Cotentin_Peninsula.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cotentin_Peninsula.jpg)

A map of the Cotentin peninsula, with Cherbourg to the north

Cherbourg is located at the northern tip of the [Cotentin Peninsula](/wiki/Cotentin_Peninsula "Cotentin Peninsula"), in the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Manche](/wiki/Manche "Manche"), of which it is a [subprefecture](/wiki/Subprefectures_in_France "Subprefectures in France"). At the time of the [1999 census](/wiki/INSEE "INSEE") the city of Cherbourg had an area of 6.91 square kilometres (2.668 sq mi), while the city of Octeville had an area of 7.35 km2 (2.838 sq mi). The largest city in the Department of Manche, it is the result of the merger of the communes of Cherbourg and Octeville. The [amalgamated](/wiki/Amalgamation_(politics) "Amalgamation (politics)") city today has an area of 14.26 km2 (5.506 sq mi). Cherbourg is situated at the mouth of the [Divette](/w/index.php?title=Divette_(river)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Divette (river) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divette_(fleuve) "fr:Divette (fleuve)")] and at the south of the bay between [Cap Lévi](/w/index.php?title=Cap_L%C3%A9vi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cap Lévi (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cap_L%C3%A9vi "fr:Cap Lévi")] to the east and [Cap de La Hague](/wiki/La_Hague "La Hague") to the west, Cherbourg-Octeville is 120 km (75 mi) from the English coast.

Cherbourg and Octeville-sur-Cherbourg once belonged to the [deanery](/wiki/Deanery "Deanery") of La Hague, delimited by the Divette. In 1786, a part of [Equeurdreville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville") joined Cherbourg, during the construction of the port, and then in 1802, a portion of Octeville. Since 1811, the "[mielles](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mielle "wikt:mielle")" [dunes] of Tourlaville, commune of the deanery of [Saire](/wiki/Val_de_Saire "Val de Saire"), are integrated into the Cherbourg territory known as the quarter of Val-de-Saire where the [Pasteur Hospital](/w/index.php?title=Centre_hospitalier_public_du_Cotentin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Centre hospitalier public du Cotentin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centre_hospitalier_public_du_Cotentin "fr:Centre hospitalier public du Cotentin")] and the Saint-Clement Church[[7]](#cite_note-8) were built. Thus, Cherbourg-Octeville lies both in La Hague and in the Val de Saire.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Like all Chantereyne and the area of the Mielles, the Cherbourg territory was reclaimed from the sea. Built at the level of the sea, the town developed at the foot of the Roule mountain (highest point of the old town) and la Fauconnière. Octeville is a former rural municipality, composed of hamlets, whose settlement extended from the 19th century and whose territory is highly urbanised since 1950, especially around the [ZUP](/w/index.php?title=Zone_%C3%A0_urbaniser_en_priorit%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zone à urbaniser en priorité (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zone_%C3%A0_urbaniser_en_priorit%C3%A9 "fr:Zone à urbaniser en priorité")] of the provinces and the university campus.

The bordering communes are [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville") to the east, [Équeurdreville-Hainneville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville") to the west, [La Glacerie](/wiki/La_Glacerie "La Glacerie") to the south and southeast, [Martinvast](/wiki/Martinvast "Martinvast") to the south, and [Nouainville](/wiki/Nouainville "Nouainville") and [Sideville](/wiki/Sideville "Sideville") to the south-west.

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Montagne-du-roule.jpg/250px-Montagne-du-roule.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montagne-du-roule.jpg)

The Montagne du Roule seen from the commercial harbour.

Located at the end of the [Armorican Massif](/wiki/Armorican_Massif "Armorican Massif"), Cherbourg retains traces of the geologic formation, deformed [granites](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and [metamorphic](/wiki/Metamorphic_rock "Metamorphic rock") [schists](/wiki/Schist "Schist") of the [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian") of [Hercynian orogeny](/wiki/Variscan_orogeny "Variscan orogeny") by the folding of the [arkoses](/wiki/Arkose "Arkose") of the [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") and Armorican sandstone and shale of the [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician"). These folds result in layers of sandstone tilted 45° towards the north-east on la Fauconniere (including "[La Roche qui pend](/w/index.php?title=La_Roche_qui_pend&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Roche qui pend (page does not exist)")" ['the hanging rock']) and the [Montagne du Roule](/w/index.php?title=Montagne_du_Roule&action=edit&redlink=1 "Montagne du Roule (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montagne_du_Roule "fr:Montagne du Roule")].[[9]](#cite_note-10) These two cliffs are due to sea erosion in the [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary"). The retreat of the sea then gave way to sand dunes and tidal marshes, destroyed by the urbanisation of the 17th and 19th centuries, identical to those of Collignon in Tourlaville.[[10]](#cite_note-pedestre-11)

These rocks in the soil have been used for centuries in several ways: Crushed granite extracted in [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville") and [arkoses](/wiki/Arkose "Arkose") of Becquet, have been used for the manufacture of rubble ([moellon](/w/index.php?title=Moellon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moellon (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/moellon "fr:moellon")]) and blocks squared for [lintels](/wiki/Lintel "Lintel"). The [greenschist](/wiki/Greenschist "Greenschist"), whose colour comes from [chlorite](/wiki/Chlorite_group "Chlorite group") and [sericite](/wiki/Sericite "Sericite"), are used mainly for roofing in Nord-Cotentin, but also masonry in Cherbourg. The Armorican [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") of the Montagne du Roule is used for rubble and rockfill. Most of the many quarries, which opened in the metropolitan area for building the [harbour wall](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour"), are now closed.[[10]](#cite_note-pedestre-11)

### Hydrography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hydrography")]

Cherbourg is bordered by the sea. The construction of the port of trade, from 1769, accompanied by the diversion of the [Divette](/w/index.php?title=Divette_(river)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Divette (river) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divette_(fleuve) "fr:Divette (fleuve)")] (the mouth of which was located at the current exit of Port Chantereyne) and the Trottebec (from the territory of Tourlaville) gathered in the *canal de retenue*, along the *Avenue de Paris* and *Rue du Val-de-Saire*.

The streams of the Bucaille and the Fay, which watered the *Croûte du Homet*, disappeared in the 18th century[[11]](#cite_note-12) during the construction of the military port.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

Cherbourg has a temperate [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"). Its maritime character causes high humidity (84%) and a strong sea wind, commonly [stormy](/wiki/Storm "Storm") but also low seasonal variations of temperature and few days of frost (7.3).[[12]](#cite_note-infoclimat-13) The combined effect of the wind and the tides can generate a rapid change of weather in a single day, with sun and rain which can be a few hours apart.[[13]](#cite_note-meteofrance-14)

The influence of the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") and the mildness of the winter allow the naturalisation of many Mediterranean and exotic plants ([mimosas](/wiki/Mimosa "Mimosa"), [palms](/wiki/Palm_(plant) "Palm (plant)"), [agaves](/wiki/Agave "Agave"), etc.) which are present in the public and private gardens of the city, despite average insolation.[[13]](#cite_note-meteofrance-14) The climate is similar to areas much further north in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and Ireland due to the moderation. Summers are far cooler than expected by French standards.

### Routes of communication and transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Routes of communication and transport")]

#### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Road")]

Historically, Cherbourg is at the western end of [Route nationale 13](/wiki/Route_nationale_13 "Route nationale 13"), which runs through the city by the "Rouges Terres" and the *Avenue de Paris*, from [La Glacerie](/wiki/La_Glacerie "La Glacerie"). In the 1990s, a deviation from the road, now European routes [E03](/wiki/European_route_E03 "European route E03") and [E46](/wiki/European_route_E46 "European route E46"), referred traffic through La Glacerie and [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville") on a three-way axis from La Glacerie, to the Penesme roundabout at Tourlaville and then a dual carriageway to a roundabout located between Collignon Beach and the *Port des Flamands*. An extension to Cherbourg is in the works, with the doubling of the bridge over the *Port des Flamands*, to ensure a continuity of the dual carriageway to the commercial port in Cherbourg.

The old [Route nationale 801](/w/index.php?title=Route_nationale_801&action=edit&redlink=1 "Route nationale 801 (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Route_nationale_801 "fr:Route nationale 801")] (reclassified as D901), which connects [Cap de la Hague](/wiki/La_Hague "La Hague") to [Barfleur](/wiki/Barfleur "Barfleur"), crosses the city from east to west.

After the completion of the bypass east of the agglomeration, a western bypass project is under study, and a 'zone' corresponding to the future final route has been selected. Similarly, upgrading to a dual carriageway for access of [Maupertus Airport](/wiki/Cherbourg_%E2%80%93_Maupertus_Airport "Cherbourg – Maupertus Airport") is envisaged.

The D650 is used to connect Cherbourg to the west coast of the Cotentin peninsula. Departing from Cherbourg, the D650 takes a southwesterly direction to [Les Pieux](/wiki/Les_Pieux "Les Pieux") and then along to join the *Côte des Isles* (the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") coast) to [Barneville-Carteret](/wiki/Barneville-Carteret "Barneville-Carteret"). In the approach to Cherbourg, this road has undergone development, in recent years, with amenities (roundabouts, traffic lights, urban development) by virtue of the [peri-urbanisation](/wiki/Peri-urbanisation "Peri-urbanisation") of the communes in its path.

With the awarding of [autoroute](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") status to the RN13 in 2006, the work of upgrading to motorway standard between Cherbourg and [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen") is being undertaken over a 10-year period.[[17]](#cite_note-memo-18) The construction work of the RN13 at the entrance of the Cherbourg agglomeration (locality *Virage des Chèvres*) was completed in early 2009.

#### Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sea")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/NormandieExpress.jpg/250px-NormandieExpress.jpg)](/wiki/File:NormandieExpress.jpg)

The [*Normandie Express*](/wiki/HSC_Normandie_Express "HSC Normandie Express") catamaran ferry at Cherbourg

See also: [Cherbourg Harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour") and [Cherbourg Naval Base](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base")

Cherbourg-Octeville is a port on the English Channel with a number of regular passenger and freight ferry services operating from the large modern ferry terminal and has a major [artificial harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour"). The following operators currently run services from the port:

* [Brittany Ferries](/wiki/Brittany_Ferries "Brittany Ferries") to [Rosslare](/wiki/Rosslare_Europort "Rosslare Europort"), [Poole](/wiki/Poole "Poole") (1 sailing daily) and [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") (up to 2 sailings daily, summer only).
* [Stena Line](/wiki/Stena_Line "Stena Line") to [Rosslare](/wiki/Rosslare_Europort "Rosslare Europort") (3 sailings weekly).
* [Irish Ferries](/wiki/Irish_Ferries "Irish Ferries") to [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") (2 sailings weekly).

Cherbourg has previously had services operated by the following operators:

* [Stena Line](/wiki/Stena_Line "Stena Line") to [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton") (up to 2 sailings daily). Withdrawn in 1996.
* [P&O Ferries](/wiki/P%26O_Ferries "P&O Ferries") to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") (up to 2 sailings daily by conventional ferry and up to 3 by fast ferry during the summer). Withdrawn in 2005 following a business review.
* [P&O Irish Sea](/wiki/P%26O_Irish_Sea "P&O Irish Sea") to [Rosslare](/wiki/Rosslare_Europort "Rosslare Europort") (up to 3 sailings weekly) and [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") (weekends only during the summer). Dublin service was withdrawn in 2004 and Rosslare service sold to Celtic Link.
* [HD Ferries](/wiki/HD_Ferries "HD Ferries") to [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey") and [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"). Operated in 2007 but cancelled in 2008 due to lack of customers.
* [Celtic Link Ferries](/wiki/Celtic_Link_Ferries "Celtic Link Ferries") to [Rosslare](/wiki/Rosslare_Europort "Rosslare Europort") (3 sailings weekly). Service sold to Stena Line.
* [Condor Ferries](/wiki/Condor_Ferries "Condor Ferries") to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") (1 sailing weekly in summer only).

The port welcomes some 30 cruise ships per year including the largest, thanks to a cruise terminal built in 2006 in the [Gare Maritime de Cherbourg](/wiki/Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg "Gare Maritime de Cherbourg"), which had opened in 1933 on the *Quai de France* next to the *Cité de la Mer*. Frequently, cruise ships that have planned for another destination have taken refuge in the port, for protection from the frequent storms.

Conventional cargo ships berth in the eastern area of the docks on the *Quai des Flamands* and *Quai des Mielles*. During the construction of the [Concorde](/wiki/Concorde "Concorde") prototypes in the 1960s, some sections built in the United Kingdom passed by ferry through Cherbourg, for transfer to Toulouse.

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rail")]

Main article: [Cherbourg station](/wiki/Cherbourg_station "Cherbourg station")

The [Paris - Cherbourg railway line](/wiki/Mantes-la-Jolie%E2%80%93Cherbourg_railway "Mantes-la-Jolie–Cherbourg railway"), operated by [Réseau Ferré de France](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_Ferr%C3%A9_de_France "Réseau Ferré de France"), ends at [Cherbourg railway station](/wiki/Gare_de_Cherbourg "Gare de Cherbourg"), which opened in 1858 and welcomes a million passengers every year.[[18]](#cite_note-19) This line continued, at the beginning of the 20th century, up to the resort of [Urville-Nacqueville](/wiki/Urville-Nacqueville "Urville-Nacqueville") and was complemented by the [*Tue-Vâques*](/w/index.php?title=Ligne_de_Cherbourg_%C3%A0_Barfleur&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ligne de Cherbourg à Barfleur (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligne_de_Cherbourg_%C3%A0_Barfleur "fr:Ligne de Cherbourg à Barfleur")] which served from Cherbourg to [Val de Saire](/wiki/Val_de_Saire "Val de Saire") between 1911 and 1950. Today, the [Intercités](/wiki/Intercit%C3%A9s "Intercités") Paris-Caen-Cherbourg line is the most profitable in its class with profit over €10 million per year despite numerous incidents and delays.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Regular services operate to [Paris-Saint-Lazare](/wiki/Paris_Saint_Lazare_railway_station "Paris Saint Lazare railway station") via [Caen](/wiki/Gare_de_Caen "Gare de Caen") using [Intercités](/wiki/Intercit%C3%A9s "Intercités") stock, local [TER](/wiki/TER_Basse_Normandie "TER Basse Normandie") services operate from the station to [Lisieux](/wiki/Gare_Principale_Lisieux "Gare Principale Lisieux") via [Caen](/wiki/Gare_de_Caen "Gare de Caen") and to [Rennes](/wiki/Gare_de_Rennes "Gare de Rennes") via [Saint-Lô](/wiki/Saint-L%C3%B4 "Saint-Lô"). Intercités services to [Paris-Saint-Lazare](/wiki/Gare_Saint-Lazare "Gare Saint-Lazare") take three hours on average.

From July 2009 to December 2010, a TGV Cherbourg – [Dijon](/wiki/Gare_de_Dijon-Ville "Gare de Dijon-Ville") service operated, via [Mantes](/wiki/Gare_de_Mantes-la-Jolie "Gare de Mantes-la-Jolie") and [Roissy TGV](/wiki/A%C3%A9roport_Charles_de_Gaulle_2_TGV "Aéroport Charles de Gaulle 2 TGV"). With one daily round-trip, it operated experimentally for three years and gave the people of Cherbourg direct access by rail to France's primary airport. The service ceased prematurely, as the minimum threshold of passenger traffic was not met.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

As well as a main line station there was also the *[Gare Maritime Transatlantique](/wiki/Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg "Gare Maritime de Cherbourg")* station. This now forms part of the *Cité de la mer*.

#### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Heuliez_GX_317_n%C2%B0814_Schuman_-_Z%C3%A9phir_Bus.JPG/250px-Heuliez_GX_317_n%C2%B0814_Schuman_-_Z%C3%A9phir_Bus.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heuliez_GX_317_n%C2%B0814_Schuman_-_Z%C3%A9phir_Bus.JPG)

A bus of the [Zéphir Bus](/w/index.php?title=Z%C3%A9phir_Bus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zéphir Bus (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Z%C3%A9phir_Bus "fr:Zéphir Bus")] network of Cherbourg.

Further information: [fr:Zéphir Bus](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Z%C3%A9phir_Bus "fr:Zéphir Bus")

The *Compagnie des transports de Cherbourg* (CTC) was created in 1896, connecting the *Place de [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville")* and the *Place du Château* by a [tramway](/w/index.php?title=Tramway_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tramway de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tramway_de_Cherbourg "fr:Tramway de Cherbourg")] in Cherbourg, then to [Urville](/wiki/Urville-Nacqueville "Urville-Nacqueville"). After the German occupation and bombardment of the tram depot, the use of buses took over, and it was not until 1962 that the network had several lines. From 1976, the *Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg* supported the jurisdiction of public transit. Management of the public service is delegated to [Keolis](/wiki/Keolis "Keolis"), the CTC took the name of [Zephir](http://zephir.actipage.net/) Bus in 1991.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The network covers the whole of the metropolitan area. In recent years, a night bus service has also been created.

Cherbourg-Octeville and its suburbs are also served by the Manéo departmental bus service.

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Air")]

The [Cherbourg – Maupertus Airport](/wiki/Cherbourg_%E2%80%93_Maupertus_Airport "Cherbourg – Maupertus Airport"), located in [Maupertus-sur-Mer](/wiki/Maupertus-sur-Mer "Maupertus-sur-Mer"), serves the city. Its 2,440 m (8,010 ft) runway hosts [charter flights](/wiki/Air_charter "Air charter"). After stopping the daily service to Paris by [Twin Jet](/wiki/Twin_Jet "Twin Jet"), in spring 2008, a new link with Caen and Paris started with [Chalair](/wiki/Chalair_Aviation "Chalair Aviation") on 27 October 2008.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

With 40,500 passengers in 2007, the airport had lost 30% of its commercial passengers, and 10% of its total traffic over a year.[[23]](#cite_note-memo2008-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [fr:Histoire de Cherbourg](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Histoire_de_Cherbourg "fr:Histoire de Cherbourg")

### Heraldry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Heraldry")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Arms of Cherbourg-Octeville](/wiki/File:Blason_ville_fr_CherbourgOcteville_(Manche).svg "Arms of Cherbourg-Octeville")  Arms of Cherbourg-Octeville | The arms of Cherbourg-Octeville are [blazoned](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon"): *[Azure](/wiki/Azure_(heraldry) "Azure (heraldry)"), on a [fess](/wiki/Fess "Fess") [argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent"), [charged](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)") of three [mullets](/wiki/Star_(heraldry) "Star (heraldry)") of six points [sable](/wiki/Sable_(heraldry) "Sable (heraldry)"), accompanied of three [bezants](/wiki/Roundel_(heraldry) "Roundel (heraldry)") ([Or](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)")), two in [chief](/wiki/Chief_(heraldry) "Chief (heraldry)"), one in [point](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)").* From the [Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"), the coat of arms was accompanied by external ornaments: *Mural crown with five rounds of argent, crest crossed fess a [caduceus](/wiki/Caduceus "Caduceus") bypassed same on which are suspended two scallops used as mantling, one dexter olive, the other sinister oak, argent knotted and fastened by strips of azure.* They also contain a [Croix de guerre 1939-1945](/wiki/Croix_de_guerre_1939%E2%80%931945_(France) "Croix de guerre 1939–1945 (France)") with natural palm, appended at the point of the shield and surmounting the croisure strips.[[24]](#cite_note-letourneur9-25) |

The origin of the coat of arms is disputed.

According to Victor Le Sens, it is of religious origin: Fess argent charged of stars represents the belt of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)"), one of the two patrons of the city and the number of stars, like the [bezants](/wiki/Bezant "Bezant"), evokes the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), the other patron of the city. The bezants would be the expression of the redemption of the captives, illustrating the participation of the notables of Cherbourg on the [Third Crusade](/wiki/Third_Crusade "Third Crusade"). The coat of arms of Cherbourg dates from the late 12th century, at the time of the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades").[[25]](#cite_note-lesens-26)

According to M. Le Poupet, which relies in particular on the works of [Vulson de la Colombière](/wiki/Marcus_Vulson_de_la_Colombi%C3%A8re "Marcus Vulson de la Colombière") and [Ségoing](/w/index.php?title=Antoine-Jean_Gros,_Baron_S%C3%A9going_de_Laborde&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antoine-Jean Gros, Baron Ségoing de Laborde (page does not exist)"), the content of the coat of arms evokes the maritime trade of the city, the bezants - traditional furniture of the arms of ennobled financiers - represent wealth and fortune, while the star shows peace and prudence. The sable signifies prudence and constancy in adversity, the azure denotes activity and the seas. M. Canel had explained before him that the bezants and stars respectively illustrated trade and sea port.[[26]](#cite_note-blason-27)

The stars, absent from the armorial of [d'Hozier](/wiki/Charles-Ren%C3%A9_d%27Hozier "Charles-René d'Hozier") in 1697,[[26]](#cite_note-blason-27) were added in the 18th century. Under the Empire, the coat of arms was completed by a free area of second-class towns which is to dexter azure to an "N" of or, surmounted by a pointed star of the same, brocading at the ninth of the escutcheon.[[25]](#cite_note-lesens-26)

Regarding the external ornaments, the mural crown symbolises protection and happiness, the caduceus of trade and business, the olive tree of peace, the oak of strength, recalling the role of both the military and commercial port. The argent means that Cherbourg was a second class city under the Empire.[[24]](#cite_note-letourneur9-25)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Arms of Octeville](/wiki/File:Blason_ville_fr_Octeville_(Manche).svg "Arms of Octeville")  Arms of Octeville | The arms of Cherbourg-Octeville are [blazoned](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon"): *[Vert](/wiki/Vert_(heraldry) "Vert (heraldry)") to the [mantle](/wiki/Mantling "Mantling") of [argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent") charged with two capital letters of sable "O" dexter, "V" sinister, of the chief of gules to a leopard or armed and [lampasséd](/w/index.php?title=Lampass%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lampassé (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/lampass%C3%A9 "fr:lampassé")] azure.* It was the logo of the municipality until the merger with Cherbourg, which then took the logo of Cherbourg. |

Today, the municipality of Cherbourg-Octeville uses a logo, entitled *"mouette musicale"* [musical seagull]. Initially adopted by Cherbourg, it consists of a gull, symbolising the maritime character of the town, on a musical stave, evoking the musicality of the port: "The cry of the seagulls that dance between sky and sea, the mermaids of ships and the melodious song of the waves".[[27]](#cite_note-28)

### Origins and toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Origins and toponymy")]

The date of Foundation of Cherbourg can not be set precisely, although several local historians, including Robert Lerouvillois, trace the origin of the city to Coriallo (for \**Coriovallo*) of the [Unelli](/wiki/Unelli "Unelli"). According to [Pierre-Yves Lambert](/wiki/Pierre-Yves_Lambert "Pierre-Yves Lambert"), the Celtic element *corio-* means "army, troop" and the element *vallo-* similar to the Latin *vallum*, would be "rampart, fortification".[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Mentioned on the *[Tabula Peutingeriana](/wiki/Tabula_Peutingeriana "Tabula Peutingeriana")* (c. 365), in the [Antonine Itinerary](/wiki/Antonine_Itinerary "Antonine Itinerary") and the *Gesta* de [Fontenelle](/wiki/Wandregisel "Wandregisel") ("In pago Coriovallinse", 747-753), *Coriallo*, Latinised then as *Coriallum*, hosted a Roman garrison during the late [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), and the recovered remains would be the village between Cherbourg and [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville"), on the Mielles.[[29]](#cite_note-fondation-30)

The Cotentin Peninsula was the first territory conquered by the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") in their ninth century invasion. They developed Cherbourg as a port. After the Anglo-Scandinavian settlement, a new name appeared there in a still Latinised form: *Carusburg Castellum* (1026-1027, Fauroux 58) then *Carisburg* (1056–1066, Fauroux 214), *Chiersburg* ([William of Jumièges](/wiki/William_of_Jumi%C3%A8ges "William of Jumièges"), v. 1070), *Chieresburg* ([Wace](/wiki/Wace "Wace"), *[Roman de Rou](/wiki/Roman_de_Rou "Roman de Rou")*, v. 1175).[[30]](#cite_note-beaurepaire-31) *Carusburg* would mean "fortress of the marsh" in [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") *kjarr* (marsh), and *[borg](/wiki/Town "Town")* (castle, fortified town)[[31]](#cite_note-32) or "city of the marais" in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *ker* (bog) and *burgh* (town). The element *kjarr* / *ker* is also found in Normandy in [Villequier](/wiki/Villequier "Villequier") and [Orcher](/wiki/Gonfreville-l%27Orcher "Gonfreville-l'Orcher"). According to François de Beaurepaire, it comes rather from the Old English *chiriche* (spelled *ċiriċe*, Church) or [tch] is reduced to [s], as the commune of [Chirbury](/wiki/Chirbury "Chirbury"), in the County of [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), formerly also spelled *Chirichburig* (915) and *Chiresbir* (1226).[[30]](#cite_note-beaurepaire-31)

The name of Octeville appears meanwhile, in 1063, in a Charter of [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") about allocations made to the Collegiate Church of Cherbourg.[[32]](#cite_note-hist-oct-33) It means: "the rural area of *Otti*", a [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") male name also found in [Octeville-l'Avenel](/wiki/Octeville-l%27Avenel "Octeville-l'Avenel"), [Octeville-sur-Mer](/wiki/Octeville-sur-Mer "Octeville-sur-Mer") and [Otby](/wiki/Walesby,_Lincolnshire "Walesby, Lincolnshire") (Lincolnshire, *Ottebi*, 11th century).

Cherbourg is also the name of a [Canadian township](/wiki/Township_(Canada) "Township (Canada)"), located between [Matane](/wiki/Matane "Matane") and [Les Méchins](/wiki/Les_M%C3%A9chins,_Quebec "Les Méchins, Quebec"), which gave its name to the communes of Saint-Thomas-de-Cherbourg, merged in 1954 into Les Méchins, and [Saint-Jean-de-Cherbourg](/wiki/Saint-Jean-de-Cherbourg,_Quebec "Saint-Jean-de-Cherbourg, Quebec"). This name, including the proclamation date of 7 May 1864, could be due to the impact by the local newspapers of the inauguration of the military port by [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") in 1858.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Cherbourg is also the name of a town in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Cherbourg_au_17e_siecle.jpg/250px-Cherbourg_au_17e_siecle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg_au_17e_siecle.jpg)

A view of Cherbourg in the 17th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Chateau-cherbourg-1689b.jpg/250px-Chateau-cherbourg-1689b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chateau-cherbourg-1689b.jpg)

Map of Cherbourg and the castle demolished in 1689.

The Cotentin, conquered by [Quintus Titurius Sabinus](/wiki/Quintus_Titurius_Sabinus "Quintus Titurius Sabinus") in 56 BC,[[34]](#cite_note-35) was divided between the *pagus constantiensis* ("County of Coutances") and the *pagus coriovallensis* ("County of Coriallo"), within [Gallia Lugdunensis](/wiki/Gallia_Lugdunensis "Gallia Lugdunensis"). Coriallo housed a small garrison and a [castrum](/wiki/Castra "Castra") was built on the left bank of the Divette as an element of the *Litus saxonicum*, after [Saxon](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") raids at the beginning of the fourth century.[[29]](#cite_note-fondation-30)

In 497, the village was sold with all of [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica") to [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I"). It was evangelised by [Saint Éreptiole](/w/index.php?title=Saint_%C3%89reptiole&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint Éreptiole (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saint_%C3%89reptiole "fr:Saint Éreptiole")] in 432, then by [Saint Exuperat](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Exuperat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint Exuperat (page does not exist)"), [Saint Leonicien](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Leonicien&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint Leonicien (page does not exist)"), and finally [Saint Scubilion](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Scubilion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint Scubilion (page does not exist)") in 555.[[35]](#cite_note-36) In 870, [Saint Clair](/w/index.php?title=Clair_du_Beauvaisis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clair du Beauvaisis (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clair_du_Beauvaisis "fr:Clair du Beauvaisis")], landing in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), was ordained priest of Cherbourg and established a hermitage in the surrounding forest.[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37)

After several [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") raids in the ninth century, Cherbourg was attached to the [Duchy of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy") along with the [Cotentin](/wiki/Cotentin_Peninsula "Cotentin Peninsula"), in 933, by [William Longsword](/wiki/William_I_Longsword "William I Longsword"). The Danish King Harold moved there in 946.

In the face of English threats, [Richard III of Normandy](/wiki/Richard_III_of_Normandy "Richard III of Normandy") strengthened the fortifications of the [castle](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Cherbourg "Château de Cherbourg") at the same time as those of the other major strongholds of Cotentin. In 1053, the city was one of the four main cities of the duchy of [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") to receive an annuity in perpetuity for the maintenance of one hundred needy.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

In 1139, during the struggle for succession to the Anglo-Norman Crown, Cherbourg fell after two months of siege to the troops of [Stephen of England](/wiki/Stephen,_King_of_England "Stephen, King of England") before being retaken in 1142 by [Geoffrey of Anjou](/wiki/Geoffrey_Plantagenet,_Count_of_Anjou "Geoffrey Plantagenet, Count of Anjou"), whose wife, [Empress Matilda](/wiki/Empress_Matilda "Empress Matilda"), three years later founded the [Abbaye Notre-Dame du Vœu](/w/index.php?title=Abbaye_Notre-Dame_du_V%C5%93u&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abbaye Notre-Dame du Vœu (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abbaye_Notre-Dame_du_V%C5%93u "fr:Abbaye Notre-Dame du Vœu")].[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37)

During the conquest of Normandy by [Philip II of France](/wiki/Philip_II_of_France "Philip II of France"), Cherbourg fell without a fight in 1204. The city was sacked in 1284 and 1293, the abbey and the Hôtel-Dieu looted and burned, but the castle, where the population was entrenched, resisted. Following these ravages, [Philip IV of France](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_France "Philip IV of France") fortified the city in 1300.[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37)

Its strategic position, a key to the kingdom along with Calais as a bridgehead for invasion by the English and French, the town was much disputed during the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). Having one of the strongest castles in the world according to [Froissart](/wiki/Jean_Froissart "Jean Froissart"), it changed ownership six times as a result of transactions or seats, never by force of arms. The fortress resisted the soldiers of [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") in 1346.

In February 1354, Cherbourg was transferred by [John II of France](/wiki/John_II_of_France "John II of France") to [Charles II of Navarre](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Navarre "Charles II of Navarre") with the bulk of the Cotentin.[[38]](#cite_note-39) The city was of Navarre from 1354 to 1378, and Charles II stayed in Cherbourg on several occasions. In 1378, the city was besieged by [Charles V of France](/wiki/Charles_V_of_France "Charles V of France") as the rest of the Norman possessions of the King of Navarre, but in vain. Navarre troops who had dropped the County of Évreux and the Cotentin were entrenched in Cherbourg, already a difficult taking, and defended it against French attacks.[[39]](#cite_note-40) In June 1378, having lost ground in Normandy, Charles II of Navarre rented Cherbourg in 1378 to [Richard II of England](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England") for a period of three years. [Bertrand du Guesclin](/wiki/Bertrand_du_Guesclin "Bertrand du Guesclin") besieged it for six months using many machines of war, but abandoned the siege in December 1378.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The King of England then refused to return the city to the Navarrese, despite the efforts of Charles II. It was only his son [Charles III of Navarre](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Navarre "Charles III of Navarre") who recovered it in 1393. In 1404, it was returned to [Charles VI of France](/wiki/Charles_VI_of_France "Charles VI of France"), in exchange for the Duchy of [Nemours](/wiki/Nemours "Nemours").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Fallen in 1418 to the hands of the English, Cherbourg, the last English possession of the Duchy of Normandy after the [Battle of Formigny](/wiki/Battle_of_Formigny "Battle of Formigny"), was retaken by France following the [Siege of Cherbourg](/wiki/Siege_of_Cherbourg_(1450) "Siege of Cherbourg (1450)") in 1450.[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37)

On 28 April 1532, Cherbourg was visited with great fanfare by [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") and the dauphin.[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37) At that time, Cherbourg was described by [Gilles de Gouberville](/wiki/Gilles_de_Gouberville "Gilles de Gouberville") as a fortified town of 4,000 residents, protected by drawbridges at the three main gates which were permanently guarded and closed from sunset until dawn. Inside the city walls, the castle, itself protected by wide moats and equipped with a keep and twelve towers, was south-east of the city. Outside and to the south of the city walls, the suburb along the Divette was frequented by sailors.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Cherbourg was not affected by the wind of the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") that divided Normandy, consolidated and heavily guarded by [Matignon](/w/index.php?title=Jacques_II_de_Goyon_de_Matignon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacques II de Goyon de Matignon (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacques_II_de_Goyon_de_Matignon "fr:Jacques II de Goyon de Matignon")], [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France") thanked his defence against the troops of [Montgomery](/wiki/Gabriel,_comte_de_Montgomery "Gabriel, comte de Montgomery"), as lieutenant-general of Normandy and Governor of Cherbourg in 1578, and then marshal the following year. The bourgeois also remained loyal to Henry III and [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France"), when Normandy was mostly held by the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)").[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37)

### 17th to 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 17th to 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Cherbourg-Digue-ouest-grande-rade.jpg/250px-Cherbourg-Digue-ouest-grande-rade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg-Digue-ouest-grande-rade.jpg)

The western sea wall, known as *"de Querqueville"*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/N_cherbourg.jpg/250px-N_cherbourg.jpg)](/wiki/File:N_cherbourg.jpg)

Napoléon and Marie Louise attending the parade of the squadron in Cherbourg in 1811.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Belle-poule-napoleon-morel-fatio.jpg/250px-Belle-poule-napoleon-morel-fatio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belle-poule-napoleon-morel-fatio.jpg)

*Transhipment of the remains of Napoleon I*, [Léon Morel-Fatio](/wiki/Antoine_L%C3%A9on_Morel-Fatio "Antoine Léon Morel-Fatio"), 1841.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/General_view_from_fort_du_Roule%2C_Cherbourg%2C_France-LCCN2001697636.jpg/250px-General_view_from_fort_du_Roule%2C_Cherbourg%2C_France-LCCN2001697636.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_view_from_fort_du_Roule,_Cherbourg,_France-LCCN2001697636.jpg)

A general view of Cherbourg from the *fort du Roule*, around 1895

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Cherbourg-Octeville_-_Mairie.jpg/250px-Cherbourg-Octeville_-_Mairie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg-Octeville_-_Mairie.jpg)

The [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Cherbourg "Hôtel de Ville, Cherbourg")

To complement the two major ports of [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France") on the Atlantic Ocean and [Toulon](/wiki/Toulon "Toulon") on the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") wished to build a new port on the side of the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), facing England, in order to shelter the passing ships. In 1686, [Vauban](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Le_Prestre_de_Vauban "Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban") offered to strengthen the fortifications of Cherbourg, and close [Cherbourg Harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour") with two sea walls, but preferred La Hogue for the establishment of a major military port.[[43]](#cite_note-bazan-44) Fortifications and the castle development work began the following year but were stopped by the King in December 1688, influenced by [Louvois](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Michel_le_Tellier,_Marquis_de_Louvois "François-Michel le Tellier, Marquis de Louvois") and fear of English attacks.[[29]](#cite_note-fondation-30) In the absence of these fortifications, the population of Cherbourg attended to the destruction of the three ships of [Admiral Tourville](/wiki/Anne_Hilarion_de_Tourville "Anne Hilarion de Tourville") at the end of the [Battle of La Hogue](/wiki/Battles_of_Barfleur_and_La_Hogue "Battles of Barfleur and La Hogue").[[29]](#cite_note-fondation-30)

The commercial port dug at the current position of the *place Divette* between 1739 and 1742, was devastated in August 1758 by an English attack under the orders of [General Bligh](/wiki/Thomas_Bligh "Thomas Bligh") and [Admiral Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe"). During the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), the British briefly occupied the town after the [Raid on Cherbourg](/wiki/Raid_on_Cherbourg "Raid on Cherbourg") in 1758. The British destroyed military buildings and warehouses before departing. With the development of a new pool of trade in 1769, Cherbourg - a longstanding commercial port of minor importance, a city without a university or cultural activity, regularly looted, and having weak relations with Paris - acquired a weight in the Cotentin which translated, on the eve of the French Revolution, by the creation of networks of sociability by the middle-class united in associations - such as the [Cherbourg Royal Academic Society](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg_Royal_Academic_Society&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cherbourg Royal Academic Society (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_nationale_acad%C3%A9mique_de_Cherbourg "fr:Société nationale académique de Cherbourg")] in 1755 and the [lodge](/wiki/Masonic_Lodge "Masonic Lodge") "Faithful mason". The population increased from 800 [feus](/wiki/Feu_fiscal "Feu fiscal") (4,000 inhabitants) in Cherbourg and 95 in Octeville, around 1715, to 7,300 people in Cherbourg by 1778.[[44]](#cite_note-ancienregime-45)

[Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") decided to relaunch the project of the port on the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). After many delays, it was decided in 1779 to build a 4 km (2.5 mi)-long sea wall between [île Pelée](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour") and the tip of [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville"), using a method developed by [Louis-Alexandre de Cessart](/wiki/Louis-Alexandre_de_Cessart "Louis-Alexandre de Cessart"), a pier of 90 wooden cones of 20 m (66 ft) by 20, filled with [rubble](/wiki/Rubble "Rubble"), connected by iron chains. The first cone was immersed on 6 June 1784, and the King attended the [launching of the ninth cone](/w/index.php?title=History_of_the_French_Navy_under_Louis_XV_and_Louis_XVI&action=edit&redlink=1 "History of the French Navy under Louis XV and Louis XVI (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Histoire_de_la_marine_fran%C3%A7aise_sous_Louis_XV_et_Louis_XVI "fr:Histoire de la marine française sous Louis XV et Louis XVI")] on 22 June. But the technique did not withstand storms and was abandoned in 1788 in favour of scuttling old warships to backfill lost stones touted by [La Bretonnière](/wiki/Louis_de_La_Couldre_de_La_Bretonni%C3%A8re "Louis de La Couldre de La Bretonnière"). However, the reduction of subsidies and the revolutionary events slowed work down, until its suspension in 1792. A new [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Cherbourg "Hôtel de Ville, Cherbourg") (town hall) was completed in 1804.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

First Consul [Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") wanted to turn Cherbourg into a major military port, for the invasion of the United Kingdom. He charged [Joseph Cachin](/wiki/Joseph_Cachin "Joseph Cachin") with the resumption of the work of the sea wall, the digging of [military outer harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base"), and the construction of the new arsenal. After a visit in 1811, Napoleon made Cherbourg a [maritime prefecture](/wiki/Maritime_Prefect "Maritime Prefect"), a *[chef-lieu](/wiki/Chef-lieu "Chef-lieu")* of the [Arrondissements of the Manche department](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_the_Manche_department "Arrondissements of the Manche department") and the seat of a [court of first instance](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court").

The work of the central sea wall, interrupted again between 1813 and 1832, ended in 1853, the east and west sea walls in 1895. The [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France") docks (begun in 1814 - 290 × 220 × 18 metres) and [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") (started in 1836 - 420 × 200 × 18 m) of the military port were respectively opened on 25 August 1829, in the presence of the Dauphin, and 7 August 1858, by the Imperial couple. The work of the sea wall was concluded by the construction of the small harbour (Homet sea wall, 1899-1914 and sea wall of the Flemings, 1921–1922).

The work of the port led the intensification and spread of a modernising and developing Cherbourg, while contractors, owners, and local merchants were getting richer. Rural village housing scattered in hamlets made up around large farms (La Crespiniere, La Prevallerie, Grimesnil, La Gamacherie, etc.), connected between them and the Saint-Martin Church by a network of paths, Octeville became chef-lieu of the canton in 1801 (Decree of 23 Vendémiaire, year X) and also its population, to increase by the influx of workers who came to build the port of Cherbourg and work at the Arsenal. After the creation of the *Route des Pieux* (current *Rue Salengro* and *Rue Carnot*), the town was formed around an homogenised street-village then urbanising at the beginning of the 20th century.[[32]](#cite_note-hist-oct-33)

On 16 August 1830, [King Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France"), dethroned, departed into exile from the military port of Cherbourg aboard the *Great Britain*, leaving room for the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy").[[36]](#cite_note-fleury1839-37) After seeing moor in its harbour *Le Luxor* carrying the [Obelisk of Luxor](/wiki/Luxor_Obelisk "Luxor Obelisk") in August 1833, Cherbourg welcomed the return of the remains of Napoleon to France aboard the *Belle Poule*. On 4 August 1858, an equestrian statue of Napoleon by the sculptor [Armand Le Véel](/wiki/Armand_Le_V%C3%A9el "Armand Le Véel"), was erected on the occasion of the visit of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") to the inauguration of the [railway line from Cherbourg to Paris](/wiki/Mantes-la-Jolie%E2%80%93Cherbourg_railway "Mantes-la-Jolie–Cherbourg railway").

On 19 June 1864, a naval engagement in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") was held off the coast of Cherbourg: The warship of the Confederates, the [CSS *Alabama*](/wiki/CSS_Alabama "CSS Alabama") was sunk by the ship of the Union [USS *Kearsarge*](/wiki/USS_Kearsarge_(1861) "USS Kearsarge (1861)") after two hours of fighting [see the [Battle of Cherbourg (1864)](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg_(1864) "Battle of Cherbourg (1864)")], under the eye of thousands of spectators, who had arrived by train for the inauguration of the casino. Visualizing the fight from a sailboat, [Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet") immortalised it in *[The Battle of the Kearsarge and the Alabama](/wiki/The_Battle_of_the_Kearsarge_and_the_Alabama "The Battle of the Kearsarge and the Alabama")*. In November 1984, the [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy") [mine hunter](/wiki/Mine_hunter "Mine hunter") *Circé* discovered a [wreck](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck") under nearly 60 m (200 ft) of water off Cherbourg. The location of the wreck (WGS84) was 49°45'147N / 001°41'708W. Captain Max Guerout later confirmed the wreck to be of the CSS *Alabama*.

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg_%281933%29.jpg/250px-Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg_%281933%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg_(1933).jpg)

The gare maritime in 1933.

From 1847, the geographical and technical properties of the port of Cherbourg attracted shipping companies linking European ports to the east coast of the United States. At the end of the 1860s, the ships of the [Royal Mail Steam Packet Company](/wiki/Royal_Mail_Steam_Packet_Company "Royal Mail Steam Packet Company") and the [Hamburg America Line](/wiki/Hamburg_America_Line "Hamburg America Line") anchored in the harbour before crossing the Atlantic.[[46]](#cite_note-annales-47) After leaving [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), England, the [RMS *Titanic*](/wiki/RMS_Titanic "RMS Titanic") made its first stop at Cherbourg on 10 April 1912,[[47]](#cite_note-48) during its maiden voyage, where an additional 274 passengers embarked. In 1913, Cherbourg received 500 ships and 70,000 passengers.[[48]](#cite_note-bulletinBN-49)

On 31 July 1909, Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II "Nicholas II") and French president [Armand Fallières](/wiki/Armand_Falli%C3%A8res "Armand Fallières") met officially in Cherbourg to reinforce the [Franco-Russian Alliance](/wiki/Franco-Russian_Alliance "Franco-Russian Alliance").

During the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), traffic was completely suspended. Cherbourg became the place of arrival for equipment and the British and American troops, and for departure on leave and injuries. The military port experienced an increase in activity, and the garrison stationed at Cherbourg was reinforced. The port infrastructures were developed to receive coal and oil required for the conflict. Traffic doubled, reaching 600,000 tons in 1918.[[48]](#cite_note-bulletinBN-49)

Transatlantic transit resumed in the aftermath of the war with the British, American and Dutch transatlantic companies. To welcome the best stopovers, the Chamber of Commerce built a deep water port, a new ferry terminal, and an area dedicated to loading, unloading and storage of goods in the field of Mielles. Cherbourg became the first port of migration in Europe, and [Cunard Line](/wiki/Cunard_Line "Cunard Line"), [White Star Line](/wiki/White_Star_Line "White Star Line") and [Red Star Line](/wiki/Red_Star_Line "Red Star Line") companies united to build the *Hôtel Atlantique* [Atlantic Hotel] intended to receive emigrants before crossing. At the same time, the downtown was renovated, especially in the architectural projects of [René Levesque](/w/index.php?title=Ren%C3%A9_Levesque_(architect)&action=edit&redlink=1 "René Levesque (architect) (page does not exist)"), Drancey and [René Levavasseur](/w/index.php?title=Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architect)&action=edit&redlink=1 "René Levavasseur (architect) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architecte) "fr:René Levavasseur (architecte)")]. However, the [1929 crisis](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") put an end to the transatlantic peak.

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Second World War")]

Main article: [Battle of Cherbourg](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg "Battle of Cherbourg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Cherbourg1944-Combat_avParis.jpg/250px-Cherbourg1944-Combat_avParis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg1944-Combat_avParis.jpg)

American soldiers engaged in street fighting, on the *Avenue de Paris*.

During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (1939–1945), the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(Wehrmacht) "German Army (Wehrmacht)") occupied the north of France and fortified the coastline against invasion. As a deep-water port, Cherbourg was of strategic importance, very heavily protected against seaborne assault.

German troops arrived in the outskirts of Cherbourg on 17 June 1940, towards the end of the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"). Two days later, the City Council declared the [city open](/wiki/Open_city "Open city"), and *[Generalmajor](/wiki/Generalmajor "Generalmajor")* [Erwin Rommel](/wiki/Erwin_Rommel "Erwin Rommel"), commander of the [7th Panzer Division](/wiki/7th_Panzer_Division_(Wehrmacht) "7th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht)"), received the surrender of the city from the hands of the maritime prefect, Vice-Admiral [Jules Le Bigot](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Le_Bigot&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jules Le Bigot (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jules_Le_Bigot "fr:Jules Le Bigot")], who had earlier destroyed submarines under construction at the arsenal and East Fort.

Four years later, Cherbourg, the only deep-water port in the region, was the primary objective of the [American troops](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") who had landed at [Utah Beach](/wiki/Utah_Beach "Utah Beach") during the [Battle of Normandy](/wiki/Operation_Overlord "Operation Overlord"). The [Battle of Cherbourg](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg "Battle of Cherbourg") was required to give the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") a point of logistic support for human resupply and material of the troops. American troops encircled the city on 21 June 1944. At the end of furious street fighting and bitter resistance from the [Fort du Roule](/wiki/Fort_du_Roule "Fort du Roule"), *[Generalleutnant](/wiki/Generalleutnant "Generalleutnant")* [Karl-Wilhelm von Schlieben](/wiki/Karl-Wilhelm_von_Schlieben "Karl-Wilhelm von Schlieben"), *[Konteradmiral](/wiki/Konteradmiral "Konteradmiral")* [Walter Hennecke](/wiki/Walter_Hennecke "Walter Hennecke") and 37,000 German soldiers surrendered on 26 July to [Major General](/wiki/Major_general_(United_States) "Major general (United States)") [Joseph Lawton Collins](/wiki/J._Lawton_Collins "J. Lawton Collins"), [Commanding General](/wiki/Commanding_officer "Commanding officer") (CG) of the [U.S. VII Corps](/wiki/VII_Corps_(United_States) "VII Corps (United States)"). After a month of [demining](/wiki/Demining "Demining") and repairs by American and French engineers, the port, completely razed by the Germans and the bombing, welcomed the first [Liberty ships](/wiki/Liberty_ship "Liberty ship") and became, until the victory of 1945, the busiest port in the world, with traffic double that of [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[49]](#cite_note-50) It was also the endpoint of the gasoline which crossed the English Channel via the underwater pipeline [PLUTO](/wiki/Operation_Pluto "Operation Pluto") (Pipe Line Under The Ocean), and the starting point of the [Red Ball Express](/wiki/Red_Ball_Express "Red Ball Express"), truck transport circuit to [Chartres](/wiki/Chartres "Chartres").

Cherbourg was returned to France by the Americans on 14 October 1945. It was cited in the Order of the Army on 2 June 1948 and received the [Croix de guerre with Palm](/wiki/Croix_de_guerre_1939%E2%80%931945_(France) "Croix de guerre 1939–1945 (France)").

### Postwar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Postwar")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Le_Redoutable.JPG/250px-Le_Redoutable.JPG)](/wiki/File:Le_Redoutable.JPG)

*Le Redoutable*, a symbol of the flourishing local economy of the postwar period, became a tourist attraction in 2002.

The wartime destruction was mainly concentrated around the military port in Cherbourg but had hit 60% of Octeville. Thanks to the urgency of the port reconstruction, economic activity resumed quickly. Cherbourg, headed by former [SFIO](/wiki/French_Section_of_the_Workers%27_International "French Section of the Workers' International") Minister [René Schmitt](/w/index.php?title=Ren%C3%A9_Schmitt&action=edit&redlink=1 "René Schmitt (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Schmitt "fr:René Schmitt")], built much social housing. The postwar boom led to the modernisation of the economy and a greater role for female employment. Under the leadership of [General de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"), Cherbourg became the hub of nuclear [ballistic missile submarine](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") construction from 1964, including the first, [*Le Redoutable*](/wiki/French_submarine_Redoutable_(S611) "French submarine Redoutable (S611)"), which was launched in 1967.[[50]](#cite_note-histoireindustrielle-51) [Félix Amiot](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Amiot "Félix Amiot")'s shipyard [Constructions Mécaniques de Normandie](/wiki/Constructions_M%C3%A9caniques_de_Normandie "Constructions Mécaniques de Normandie"), specialised in military armaments, became famous during the Christmas of 1969 in an episode of the [Cherbourg Project](/wiki/Cherbourg_Project "Cherbourg Project").

Incorporated in 1970, the [Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg](/w/index.php?title=Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg "fr:Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg")] gathered together Cherbourg and Octeville, [La Glacerie](/wiki/La_Glacerie "La Glacerie"), [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville"), [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville") and [Équeurdreville-Hainneville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville").

From the end of the 1960s, the nuclear industry emerged through the construction sites of the [La Hague reprocessing plant](/wiki/La_Hague_site "La Hague site") and the [Flamanville Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Flamanville_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Flamanville Nuclear Power Plant") in addition to submarines of the [DCN](/wiki/DCNS_(company) "DCNS (company)"). A union of trade unions, left-wing activists and environmentalists, formed around the fear of the "nuclearisation" of Nord-Cotentin, crystallised in January 1979 when the *Pacific Fisher* landed with the first spent nuclear waste from Japan. On the eve of the 1980s, the Cherbourg agglomeration was hit by several violent social conflicts, particularly due to the closure of the Babcock factories.[[51]](#cite_note-cite-inquiete-52)

### Turn of the millennium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Turn of the millennium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Vue_panoramique_sur_Cherbourg_depuis_le_Fort_du_Roule_03.jpg/250px-Vue_panoramique_sur_Cherbourg_depuis_le_Fort_du_Roule_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vue_panoramique_sur_Cherbourg_depuis_le_Fort_du_Roule_03.jpg)

A recent view of downtown Cherbourg.

The major decisions of the public authorities, on which Cherbourg has depended for many centuries, and the nuclear industry, caused a deep economic crisis in the 1990s. The Arsenal was drastically downsized, the Northern Fleet (FLONOR) moved to [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France") in 1992, and the [maritime hospital](/w/index.php?title=H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas "fr:Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas")] closed. UIE, Burty, CMN, Socoval and Alcatel accumulated social plans or closings. Under the auspices of the [urban community](/w/index.php?title=Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg "fr:Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg")], the agglomeration developed its academic offerings with the [IUT of Cherbourg-Manche](/w/index.php?title=Institut_universitaire_de_technologie_de_Cherbourg-Manche&action=edit&redlink=1 "Institut universitaire de technologie de Cherbourg-Manche (page does not exist)"), the [School of Engineers of Cherbourg](/wiki/Engineering_School_of_the_University_of_Caen_Normandy_(ESIX_Normandie) "Engineering School of the University of Caen Normandy (ESIX Normandie)") and a branch of the [University of Caen](/wiki/University_of_Caen_Lower_Normandy "University of Caen Lower Normandy"), which complemented [INTECHMER](/w/index.php?title=Institut_national_des_sciences_et_techniques_de_la_mer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Institut national des sciences et techniques de la mer (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Institut_national_des_sciences_et_techniques_de_la_mer "fr:Institut national des sciences et techniques de la mer")] and the School of Fine Arts.

The new millennium began with the creation of a new commune. Cherbourg-Octeville was created on 1 March 2000 through the joining of Cherbourg and Octeville, following a local referendum within *"Grand Cherbourg"*. The city revived its tourist and maritime identity through the [Cité de la Mer](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_de_la_Mer "Cité de la Mer") and the opening to the public of the [*Redoubtable*](/wiki/Redoutable-class_submarine_(1967) "Redoutable-class submarine (1967)"), and became the home of stopovers for cruises and nautical events.[[51]](#cite_note-cite-inquiete-52) The [urban renewal operation](/w/index.php?title=Op%C3%A9ration_de_r%C3%A9novation_urbaine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Opération de rénovation urbaine (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Op%C3%A9ration_de_r%C3%A9novation_urbaine "fr:Opération de rénovation urbaine")] "between land and sea", with an emphasis on the commercial and touristic attractiveness of the city and the Bassins Quarter, as well as the economic specialisation in boating, emerged. Meanwhile, the traditional activities of the port (passengers, freight and fishing) were in crisis.[[52]](#cite_note-lesechosfr-53)

### Miscellany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Miscellany")]

The [Norman language](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language") writer [Alfred Rossel](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Rossel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alfred Rossel (page does not exist)"), a native of Cherbourg, composed many songs which form part of the heritage of the region. Rossel's song "Sus la mér" ("on the sea") is often sung as a regional patriotic song. The local dialect is known as [Cotentinais](/wiki/Cotentinais "Cotentinais").

La Glacerie was named for [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") [factory](/wiki/Factory "Factory"). In 1655, [Louis Lucas de Néhou](/w/index.php?title=Louis_Lucas_de_N%C3%A9hou&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis Lucas de Néhou (page does not exist)") built a glass factory which produced windows and mirrors for such buildings as the [Galerie des Glaces](/wiki/Galerie_des_Glaces "Galerie des Glaces") and [Château de Versailles](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Versailles "Château de Versailles"). The factory in La Glacerie was destroyed by Allied bombardments in 1944 during the Normandy invasion.

Cherbourg was the first site outside the United States to be designated as an [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") Heritage Site by the [Civil War Preservation Trust](/wiki/Civil_War_Trust "Civil War Trust") because a sea battle was fought nearby in 1864 by Union and Confederate warships. See the [Battle of Cherbourg (1864)](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg_(1864) "Battle of Cherbourg (1864)").

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Population")]

### Urban fabric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Urban fabric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Cherbourg_au_16e_siecle.jpg/250px-Cherbourg_au_16e_siecle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg_au_16e_siecle.jpg)

Plan of the fortress of Cherbourg by [Jacques Gomboust](/w/index.php?title=Jacques_Gomboust&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacques Gomboust (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacques_Gomboust "fr:Jacques Gomboust")], 1657

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Cherbourg-rue_des_fosses.jpg/250px-Cherbourg-rue_des_fosses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg-rue_des_fosses.jpg)

The *Rue des Fossés*, remnant of a street of medieval Cherbourg

Cherbourg originally developed on the left bank of the mouth of the [Divette](/w/index.php?title=Divette_(river)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Divette (river) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divette_(fleuve) "fr:Divette (fleuve)")], around the castle. Traces of the ancient fortress are rare in the modern city; the fortification was located in the area bounded by the *Rue de la Marine*, *Quai de Caligny*, the *Foch*, *Gambetta*, *Albert-Mahieu* and *François-Lavieille* streets, and *La République* and *La Trinité* squares. The city had five streets: *Grande Rue*, *Rue de la Trinité* (today, Tour-Carrée), the *Rue du Nouet* (to the Blé), the *Rue au Fourdray* and *Rue Onfroy* (of trade), and a dozen boëls (alleys).[[53]](#cite_note-anecdotique-54) These five medieval streets were transformed into pedestrian streets in the 1980s. Until the destruction of the city walls, the main road called *rue de-devant-le-château*, was built on its west (east is bordered by ditches) with several houses with arcades, called *soliers*. After the dismantling of the walls, inside which lived three-fifths of the population,[[54]](#cite_note-55) the city extended up to its natural boundaries at the end of the 17th century: the Divette in the east, and Chantereine stream in the west. During the 19th century, it extended to the neighbouring annexed territories of [Tourlaville](/wiki/Tourlaville "Tourlaville") and [Équeurdreville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville"). Its rapid growth from the end of the 18th century was spoken of by Jean Fleury, in 1839, in that it *"offers almost everywhere the appearance of a new town; the old streets occupy little space, and the others are generally large and airy, the fountains numerous [...]. Cherbourg has 10 squares, 59 streets, 12 cul-de-sacs and 5 passages."*[[55]](#cite_note-fleury1839-notions-56)

Damaged during all eras, rebuilt in piecemeal, the city has no architectural unity. Shale, extracted from the quarries of the agglomeration, is the traditional material of construction. With widespread coverage in the northern Cotentin, it is also used in Cherbourg for the walls in the city, apparent or often covered with a grayish or sometimes colourful coating. The frames are then Valognes stone (limestone), pink granite of Fermanville, or brick, and the underpinnings Armorican sandstone of the Roule and the Fauconniere. The expansion of the city from the 18th century contributed to the diversity of materials. The use of [Caen stone](/wiki/Caen_stone "Caen stone") and industrial brick was necessary under the Second Empire, while vernacular architecture disappeared gradually in these years in favour of a more homogeneous and Parisian style.[[56]](#cite_note-centreurbainancien-57)

Cherbourg and its agglomeration urbanised around the ports and along the coast. With post-war reconstruction and the economic development of the [Trente Glorieuses](/wiki/Trente_Glorieuses "Trente Glorieuses"), the city is experiencing a crisis of housing due to the demographic boom, having built on the last vacant land. Indeed, a 1954 report evaluated 1,000 inhabitant families living in slums and called for 1,500 housing units. Then out of land *Cité du Casino* in 1957 and the *Cité Fougère* in 1958, then in 1959 all of the *Amont-Quentin*, *Charcot-Spanel* and *Cité Chantereyne* to accommodate the families of the engineers and officers of the Arsenal.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

Port Chantereyne and the Mielles lands are reclaimed from the sea, the *Place Divette* and *Boulevard Schuman* are created at the site of the old fairground. However, at that time, the change mainly affected nearby villages that formed an agglomeration in less than forty years. Octeville, a dispersed habitat until the 18th century, and urbanised during the work of the port around a central street,[[32]](#cite_note-hist-oct-33) saw the [housing estate](/wiki/Housing_estate "Housing estate") of the Provinces settle on the heights of la Fauconniere and triple its population in 20 years. Several estates also emerging at Tourlaville, La Glacerie, Querquerville and Equeurdreville, amending the physiognomy of a suburb which densified.[[58]](#cite_note-59) This urbanisation resulted in the dilution of the geographic and sociological boundaries of the agglomeration resulting in the creation in 1970 of the [urban community](/w/index.php?title=Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg "fr:Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg")] until the merger of Cherbourg and Octeville in 2000.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Les-Provinces.jpg/250px-Les-Provinces.jpg)](/wiki/File:Les-Provinces.jpg)

The Amont Quentin and the Provinces, born in the 1960s and 1970s, currently under renovation.

Following this merger, a plan of urban renewal named "Between Land and Sea" was launched in 2002 on the quarters of Bassins, of the Amont-Quentin and the Provinces to homogenise the territory of the newly alamgamated city.[[59]](#cite_note-manoeuvres-60) The Bassins quarter, released by the channelling of the Divette and the filling of the retaining channel, is expected to profoundly transform the commercial landscape of the city, carried by the construction of a new shopping centre and the renovation of downtown. On the heights, seven HLM tower blocks are intended for demolition to improve social housing. A 3-star hotel and the relocation of the casino is also planned.[[60]](#cite_note-61) At *Avenue Carnot*, the former Grouard warehouses must leave room for parking and a place through from the wharf from the *Quai de l'Entrepôt* to the Pasteur Hospital, to 180 dwellings by Presqu'île habitat and ADIM (Vinci company) then 100 extra in a second round of development.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

The administrative quarters are:

* Downtown, historic heart of Cherbourg, with the inner city and the districts of La Polle and the Vœu, dating from the 19th century.
* The Val-de-Saire, annexed in 1811, beyond the Divette and swing-bridge.
* Sud-est, corresponding to the districts of du Roule and Maupas, traditionally for workers.
* The Amont Quentin-Provinces, on the heights of the city, built from the late 1950s (essentially HLM tower blocks).
* Octeville-Bourg, from both sides of the Salengro and Barbusse streets.
* Ouest, western part of the former municipality of Octeville.

Since 1996, Cherbourg-Octeville is covered by a [sensitive urban zone](/wiki/Sensitive_urban_zone "Sensitive urban zone") on the expanded area of the Provinces.

### Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Demography")]

The construction of the dam and the military port has brought an important flow of workers and soldiers. Cherbourg and Octeville have seen their populations quadruple in a century. Cherbourg had 43,000 inhabitants at the beginning of the 20th century. During this century, Cherbourg lost some 15,000 inhabitants, while Octeville grew continuously, with an explosion in the 1960s and 1970s, during the construction of the [housing estates](/wiki/Housing_estate "Housing estate").

According to estimates from [INSEE](/wiki/Institut_national_de_la_statistique_et_des_%C3%A9tudes_%C3%A9conomiques "Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques") for 2018, Cherbourg-Octeville has 35,545 inhabitants.[[62]](#cite_note-pop2018-63) It is the largest city of the Manche department, and second of [Lower Normandy](/wiki/Lower_Normandy "Lower Normandy") (after [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen")), surpassing [Alençon](/wiki/Alen%C3%A7on "Alençon"), which had been second before the amalgamation. Cherbourg concentrated 7.7% of the departmental population, twice as much as the prefecture, [Saint-Lô](/wiki/Saint-L%C3%B4 "Saint-Lô"), while the agglomeration represents 17% and the urban area 23.5%.

The depopulation of the city-centre of the agglomeration was one of the main topics of the campaign for the municipal elections of 2008. In addition to the battle of figures on the number of lost inhabitants, the three candidates, Bernard Cazeneuve (PS), Jean Lemière (UMP) and Hervé Corbin (dissident UMP) indicated a new interest in this problem. The urbanisation of the Grimesnil/Monturbet zone, provided for the coming years, should logically bring extra population, although no one knows if it will be enough to stop the demographic bleeding.
