### Others linked to Cherbourg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Others linked to Cherbourg")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Napoleon_a_Cherbourg_bordercropped.jpg/250px-Napoleon_a_Cherbourg_bordercropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Napoleon_a_Cherbourg_bordercropped.jpg)

Statue of Napoleon at Cherbourg.

The work of the seawall and the military port in Cherbourg led many soldiers and engineers, for whom this step was often an important moment in their career. Thus, [Charles François Dumouriez](/wiki/Charles_Fran%C3%A7ois_Dumouriez "Charles François Dumouriez") (1739–1823), Governor of Cherbourg who was responsible for the first work, at the dawn of the French Revolution, [Joseph Cachin](/wiki/Joseph_Cachin "Joseph Cachin") (1757–1825), engineer assigned by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") to the general direction of the maritime work of Cherbourg in 1804. For twenty years, he realised the improvement of the commercial port, and the digging of the docks of the [military port](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base"), constituting the New Arsenal. [Henri Rieunier](/wiki/Henri_Rieunier "Henri Rieunier") (1833–1918) who was twice major of the Navy in Cherbourg (1872/1875) and [Louis-Émile Bertin](/wiki/Louis-%C3%89mile_Bertin "Louis-Émile Bertin") (1840–1924) who lived in Cherbourg from 1863 to 1879, and is buried in the cemetery of [La Glacerie](/wiki/La_Glacerie "La Glacerie"). [Charles-Eugène Delaunay](/wiki/Charles-Eug%C3%A8ne_Delaunay "Charles-Eugène Delaunay") (1816–1872), Director of the [Paris Observatory](/wiki/Paris_Observatory "Paris Observatory"), drowned while visiting the harbour. Among the engineers of the [Directorate of construction and naval weapons](/wiki/DCNS_(company) "DCNS (company)"), included [Augustin-Louis Cauchy](/wiki/Augustin-Louis_Cauchy "Augustin-Louis Cauchy") (1789–1857) and also [Maxime Laubeuf](/wiki/Maxime_Laubeuf "Maxime Laubeuf"). Under the [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"), the safeguarding of the Château de Cherbourg was already the task of illustrious figures of the Kingdom, such as [Pierre des Essarts](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_des_Essarts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre des Essarts (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_des_Essarts "fr:Pierre des Essarts")], the [family of Matignon](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Goyon&action=edit&redlink=1 "House of Goyon (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maison_de_Goyon "fr:Maison de Goyon")] and [Jacques de Callières](/wiki/Jacques_de_Calli%C3%A8res "Jacques de Callières") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacques_de_Calli%C3%A8res "fr:Jacques de Callières")] (d. 1697). At the origin of the military port, [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") (1769–1821), who visited the city in 1811, *"Revient"* in Cherbourg in 1840 during the [return of his remains](/wiki/Retour_des_cendres "Retour des cendres") to France, aboard [*Belle Poule*](/wiki/French_frigate_Belle_Poule_(1834) "French frigate Belle Poule (1834)"), before being taken to [Les Invalides](/wiki/Les_Invalides "Les Invalides").

A transatlantic port of the 20th century, Cherbourg saw Hollywood stars arrive, such as [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), who organised his disembarkation in 1952 to a press conference in the *gare maritime*, critical of the [McCarthyist](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") America that he left. The port saw a lot of famous people, including businessman [Benjamin Guggenheim](/wiki/Benjamin_Guggenheim "Benjamin Guggenheim") (1865–1912) for his fatal voyage on the [Titanic](/wiki/RMS_Titanic "RMS Titanic"). Cinema then gave Cherbourg another lasting reputation, through the images of [Jacques Demy](/wiki/Jacques_Demy "Jacques Demy") (1931–1990) and music by [Michel Legrand](/wiki/Michel_Legrand "Michel Legrand") (1932-2019), in *[The Umbrellas of Cherbourg](/wiki/The_Umbrellas_of_Cherbourg "The Umbrellas of Cherbourg")*. Earlier, [Frida Boccara](/wiki/Frida_Boccara "Frida Boccara") (1940–1996), knew great success in 1961 with her song *Cherbourg avait raison*. The letters were not left with [academician](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_fran%C3%A7aise "Académie française") [Georges Grente](/wiki/Georges-Fran%C3%A7ois-Xavier-Marie_Grente "Georges-François-Xavier-Marie Grente") (1872–1959), superior of the Saint-Paul Institute, and [Ernest Psichari](/wiki/Ernest_Psichari "Ernest Psichari") (1883–1914), soldier and writer, whose garrison stay in Cherbourg in 1914 with the 2nd colonial artillery regiment inspires *L'Appel des armes*.

## Culture and heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Culture and heritage")]

### Cultural facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Cultural facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Cherbourg_Vox.jpg/250px-Cherbourg_Vox.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg_Vox.jpg)

The *Vox*, a former cinema converted into a theatre, depends upon the National Stage.

With [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), Cherbourg-Octeville is the main cultural centre of [Lower Normandy](/wiki/Lower_Normandy "Lower Normandy").

The city is the seat of several [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society"), including the [National Academic Society of Cherbourg](/w/index.php?title=National_Academic_Society_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Academic Society of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_nationale_acad%C3%A9mique_de_Cherbourg "fr:Société nationale académique de Cherbourg")] founded in 1755, [National Society of Natural Sciences and Mathematics of Cherbourg](/w/index.php?title=National_Society_of_Natural_Sciences_and_Mathematics_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Society of Natural Sciences and Mathematics of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_nationale_des_sciences_naturelles_et_math%C3%A9matiques_de_Cherbourg "fr:Société nationale des sciences naturelles et mathématiques de Cherbourg")] formed in 1851, and the Artistic and Industrial society of Cherbourg, incorporated in 1871.

The creation and dissemination of the performing arts are ensured by the [*Trident*](/wiki/Le_Trident_(theatre) "Le Trident (theatre)"), [national stage](/w/index.php?title=Sc%C3%A8ne_nationale&action=edit&redlink=1 "Scène nationale (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sc%C3%A8ne_nationale "fr:Scène nationale")] of Italian theatre, the theatre of Octeville and the *Vox*. Amateur theatre is celebrated by *Les Téméraires*.

The *vocation prioritaire du Centre régional des arts du cirque* [Priority Mission of the Regional Centre of Circus Arts] (CRAC) of La Brèche, opened in October 2006, is the residence of circus troops, but instead also offers programming for the public.[[138]](#cite_note-offreculturelle-139) CRAC participates in the festival of street arts, *Charivarue*.

In addition, the provision of artistic education is rich, with the Film Industry Institute of Normandy, the school of fine arts and the municipal music school, labelled as a conservatory for communal influence, which has 800 registrants.

After the closure of *Ultrasound* in [Équeurdreville-Hainneville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville"), a unique room of contemporary music of Nord-Cotentin, several associations have come together within the network *"La Voix des oreilles"* ["The voice of the ears"] and of the place *Épicentre*, in the former yacht club on *Quai Lawton-Collins*,[[139]](#cite_note-140) where the festival *La Terra Trema* takes place.

However, the city lacks a large capacity venue, the theatre can accommodate only 700 spectators. After the bitter failure of *Cherbourg-Land*, this problem has not been resolved at the level of the Cotentin.[[138]](#cite_note-offreculturelle-139) The Great Hall of the Cité de la Mer, with a space for over 6,000 people, has hosted several concerts, but it is primarily dedicated to the organisation of fairs and exhibitions. Today, the main welcoming complex of large-scale concerts is the Jean-Jaurès Centre of Équeurdreville-Hainneville.

Octeville has retained its patronal festival, the Sainte-Échelle, with holiday fair and parades. Cherbourg restarted its carnival in the 1980s, heir of the [Confrérie des Conards](/w/index.php?title=Confr%C3%A9rie_des_Conards&action=edit&redlink=1 "Confrérie des Conards (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confr%C3%A9rie_des_Conards "fr:Confrérie des Conards")], similar to that of [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen") and [Évreux](/wiki/%C3%89vreux "Évreux").

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Jacques-Louis_David_Patrocle.jpg/250px-Jacques-Louis_David_Patrocle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques-Louis_David_Patrocle.jpg)

The *Patroclus* of David, displayed at the Thomas-Henry Museum.

Cherbourg has several museums.

The former home of [Emmanuel Liais](/wiki/Emmanuel_Liais "Emmanuel Liais"), mayor of Cherbourg, astronomer and explorer, houses since 1905 the Museum of Natural History and Ethnography, the oldest museum in Cherbourg (founded in 1832), with curio cabinet, collection of stuffed animals, fossils, minerals and ethnographic objects (Egypt, Asia, Oceania, America and Africa), archaeological treasures and library science. It is also the headquarters of the [National Society of Natural Sciences and Mathematics of Cherbourg](/w/index.php?title=National_Society_of_Natural_Sciences_and_Mathematics_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Society of Natural Sciences and Mathematics of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_nationale_des_sciences_naturelles_et_math%C3%A9matiques_de_Cherbourg "fr:Société nationale des sciences naturelles et mathématiques de Cherbourg")].

The [Thomas-Henry Fine Arts Museum](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Thomas-Henry "Musée Thomas-Henry"), named after [its first patron](/wiki/Thomas_Henry_(patron_of_the_arts) "Thomas Henry (patron of the arts)"), was inaugurated in 1835 and is now the third collection of Normandy with 300 paintings and sculptures from the 15th to the 20th century. Located in the cultural centre, at the back of the theatre, it presents paintings by French, Flemish, Spanish and Italian artists, as well as sculptures. Presentations of works by [Fra Angelico](/wiki/Fra_Angelico "Fra Angelico"), [Simon Vouet](/wiki/Simon_Vouet "Simon Vouet"), [Camille Claudel](/wiki/Camille_Claudel "Camille Claudel"), and one of the largest collections of works by [Jean-François Millet](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Millet "Jean-François Millet"), as well as paintings by [Guillaume Fouace](/wiki/Guillaume_Fouace "Guillaume Fouace") native of [Réville](/wiki/R%C3%A9ville "Réville") or the Navy painters. Sculptures by [Armand Le Véel](/wiki/Armand_Le_V%C3%A9el "Armand Le Véel") are also included.

The Museum of the War and the Liberation, the first of its kind when it was inaugurated by [René Coty](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Coty "René Coty") on 6 June 1954, traces the daily life of Cherbourg civilians during the Occupation and the course of the Liberation of Cotentin, particularly the [Battle of Cherbourg](/wiki/Battle_of_Cherbourg "Battle of Cherbourg"). It is installed in the Fort du Roule, centrepiece of the defence of Cherbourg taken by the Americans on 25 June 1944 .

The [Cité de la Mer](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_de_la_Mer "Cité de la Mer"), is a large museum devoted to scientific and historical aspects of maritime subjects. Dedicated to oceanographic exploration, it is a complex installed since 2003 in a part of the remains of the old transatlantic station. It offers giant aquariums, a collection of underwater vehicles, such as those of [COMEX](/wiki/Compagnie_maritime_d%27expertises "Compagnie maritime d'expertises"), the [bathyscaphe](/wiki/Bathyscaphe "Bathyscaphe") *Archimède* and [*Redoutable*](/wiki/French_submarine_Redoutable_(S611) "French submarine Redoutable (S611)"), the first French [SSBN](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") built in Cherbourg, fully open to the public.

The [*Point du jour*](/w/index.php?title=Point_du_jour_(Cherbourg-Octeville)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Point du jour (Cherbourg-Octeville) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Point_du_jour_(Cherbourg-Octeville) "fr:Le Point du jour (Cherbourg-Octeville)")], a unique contemporary art centre in France, dedicated to photography, was inaugurated in the Bassin zone in November 2008.[[138]](#cite_note-offreculturelle-139)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Jeanniot_C%C3%A9lestine.jpg/250px-Jeanniot_C%C3%A9lestine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeanniot_C%C3%A9lestine.jpg)

Célestine, of the *Journal d’une femme de chambre* [*Diary of a Chambermaid*], by Georges Jeanniot, *Le Cri de Paris*, 18 November 1900.

The Jacques Prévert Municipal Library, founded in 1831 and opened in 1832, holds the second largest collection in the region, after that of Caen. The purchase of the library of the local scholar Henri-François Duchevreuil, in 1830, complements the 1,855 volumes of the district's library, created at 24 *Rue Tour-Carrée*, in application of the decree of the Convention of 8 pluviôse year II[[140]](#cite_note-histbiblio-141) and composed essentially of works seized from emigrants and deportees.[[141]](#cite_note-ccfr-142) Several donations were then made, including a legacy of 3,000 works by [Augustin Asselin](/w/index.php?title=Augustin_Asselin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Augustin Asselin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustin_Asselin "fr:Augustin Asselin")] in 1844 (with twenty-six [incunabula](/wiki/Incunable "Incunable") and a ninth-century manuscript [*De bello iudaico*](/wiki/The_Jewish_War "The Jewish War") [The Jewish War] of [Flavius Josephus](/wiki/Josephus "Josephus"), which remains the oldest document in the library) and a gift in 1877 from Jérôme-Frédéric Bignon, Mayor of [Le Rozel](/wiki/Le_Rozel "Le Rozel") and heir to the king's librarians. It also has a Norman [fonds](/wiki/Fonds "Fonds"), an old fonds devoted to botany and another to travel.[[140]](#cite_note-histbiblio-141) Housed in a wing of the City Hall in 1855, and then at 9 *Rue Thiers* (*Rue Talluau*) from 1896, the library moved into the cultural centre in June 1981, taking the name of [Jacques Prévert](/wiki/Jacques_Pr%C3%A9vert "Jacques Prévert"), who had died four years earlier in [La Hague](/wiki/La_Hague "La Hague").[[141]](#cite_note-ccfr-142) The library also participates in the *Normannia* project of the Norman digital library.

The former barracks of the Abbey, dating back to the start of the works of the large dam in 18th-century, has housed one of the five regional centres of history of the National Navy, alongside [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France"), [Lorient](/wiki/Lorient "Lorient"), [Rochefort](/wiki/Rochefort,_Charente-Maritime "Rochefort, Charente-Maritime") and [Toulon](/wiki/Toulon "Toulon"), since 1970. The archives of the maritime district of the [Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") are grouped here, and the library of the Navy founded in 1836 in Cherbourg and specialising in maritime history with its 23,000 works.

Each year, a network of writing workshops are organised in the metropolitan area, the *Mercurielles*, and the Festival of the Book and of Youth Comic Books (since 1987).

The Biennial of the 9th Art exhibits the works of [comic](/wiki/Comics "Comics") cartoonists ([Enki Bilal](/wiki/Enki_Bilal "Enki Bilal") in 2002, [François Schuiten](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Schuiten "François Schuiten") and [Benoît Peeters](/wiki/Beno%C3%AEt_Peeters "Benoît Peeters") in 2004, [André Juillard](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Juillard "André Juillard") in 2006, and [Loustal](/wiki/Jacques_de_Loustal "Jacques de Loustal") in 2008). In 2002, with the support of the City Hall of Cherbourg, Enki Bilal had planned to create a mural in the old *gare maritime* to represent the history of migration in the place; This project was rejected on the basis of an official complaint by [Bernard Cauvin](/w/index.php?title=Bernard_Cauvin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bernard Cauvin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernard_Cauvin "fr:Bernard Cauvin")], president of the [CUC](/w/index.php?title=Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_urbaine_de_Cherbourg "fr:Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg")] and the [Cité de la Mer](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_de_la_Mer "Cité de la Mer").[[142]](#cite_note-143)

Cherbourg-Octeville is the seat of two publishing houses, *Isoète* founded in 1985 and [*Le Point du jour*](/w/index.php?title=Le_Point_du_jour_(Cherbourg-Octeville)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Point du jour (Cherbourg-Octeville) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Point_du_jour_(Cherbourg-Octeville) "fr:Le Point du jour (Cherbourg-Octeville)")] established in 1996.

#### Cherbourg-Octeville in literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Cherbourg-Octeville in literature")]

* [Honoré de Balzac](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_de_Balzac "Honoré de Balzac") cites the engineer Joseph Cachin, constructor of the port of Cherbourg, among the men of genius in *[Le Curé de village](/wiki/Le_Cur%C3%A9_de_village "Le Curé de village")* [The Village Priest] and *[La Duchesse de Langeais](/wiki/La_Duchesse_de_Langeais "La Duchesse de Langeais")* [The Duchess of Langeais].[[143]](#cite_note-144) Cherbourg is also present in *[Le Réquisitionnaire](/wiki/Le_R%C3%A9quisitionnaire "Le Réquisitionnaire")* [The Recruit].[[144]](#cite_note-145)
* [Octave Mirbeau](/wiki/Octave_Mirbeau "Octave Mirbeau"), [*Le Journal d'une femme de chambre*](/wiki/The_Diary_of_a_Chambermaid_(novel) "The Diary of a Chambermaid (novel)") [The Diary of a Chambermaid], 1900: In the final chapter, Celestine became the owner of a cafe in Cherbourg
* [Remy de Gourmont](/wiki/Remy_de_Gourmont "Remy de Gourmont"), *Un cœur virginal* [A Virginal Heart], 1907
* [Ernest Psichari](/wiki/Ernest_Psichari "Ernest Psichari"), *L'Appel des armes* [The Call to Arms], 1913
* Gilles Rosset, *Le Vent dominant* [The Prevailing Wind], Grasset, 1979
* [Alexis Salatko](/wiki/Alexis_Salatko "Alexis Salatko"), *Vingt deux nuances de gris* [Twenty-two Shades of Grey], 1990
* [Jean-Philippe Arrou-Vignod](/wiki/Jean-Philippe_Arrou-Vignod "Jean-Philippe Arrou-Vignod"), *L’omelette au sucre* [The Omelette with Sugar], Gallimard, Folio Junior, 1999
* Robert Sinsoilliez, *Une balle pour rien à Cherbourg* [A Bullet for Nothing to Cherbourg], 2000
* Salatko, Alexis (2007). *Un fauteuil au bord du vide* [*A Chair at the Edge of the Vacuum*] (in French). Paris: Fayard. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-2-213-62615-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-2-213-62615-4 "Special:BookSources/978-2-213-62615-4").
* Ken Follett's novel *[The Pillars of the Earth](/wiki/The_Pillars_of_the_Earth "The Pillars of the Earth")* features Cherbourg as the hometown of Jacques Cherbourg, a Frenchman washed ashore in England during the [European Middle Ages](/wiki/European_Middle_Ages "European Middle Ages"). His son, Jack Jackson, travels to France as an adult and meets his father's family in Cherbourg.
* Kimberly Brubaker Bradley set her novel, *[For Freedom: The Story of a French Spy](/w/index.php?title=For_Freedom:_The_Story_of_a_French_Spy&action=edit&redlink=1 "For Freedom: The Story of a French Spy (page does not exist)")* in Cherbourg. The narrator, Suzanne Hall (née David), is a spy for the [French Resistance](/wiki/French_Resistance "French Resistance").
* Cherbourg (or to be precise its analog in the [Lord Darcy](/wiki/Lord_Darcy_(character) "Lord Darcy (character)") universe) is the setting for [Randall Garrett](/wiki/Randall_Garrett "Randall Garrett")'s short story "A Case of Identity" and is part of the backdrop for his novel *[Too Many Magicians](/wiki/Too_Many_Magicians "Too Many Magicians")*.

### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Cinema")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/L%27OMNIA_se_trouve_rue_de_la_Paix%2C_derri%C3%A8re_l%27H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_Cherbourg_1944.jpg/250px-L%27OMNIA_se_trouve_rue_de_la_Paix%2C_derri%C3%A8re_l%27H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_Cherbourg_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27OMNIA_se_trouve_rue_de_la_Paix,_derri%C3%A8re_l%27H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_Cherbourg_1944.jpg)

The *Omnia* cinema in 1944, after the Liberation.

The cinema occupies a significant place in the life of Cherbourg. Many classics of French cinema have been filmed there, such as *[La Marie du port](/wiki/Marie_of_the_Port_(film) "Marie of the Port (film)")* directed by [Marcel Carné](/wiki/Marcel_Carn%C3%A9 "Marcel Carné") and starring [Jean Gabin](/wiki/Jean_Gabin "Jean Gabin"). In 1981, [Claude Miller](/wiki/Claude_Miller "Claude Miller") also located the action film *[Garde à Vue](/wiki/Garde_%C3%A0_Vue "Garde à Vue")* there, though shot in the studio. However, the most emblematic is undoubtedly *[The Umbrellas of Cherbourg](/wiki/The_Umbrellas_of_Cherbourg "The Umbrellas of Cherbourg")* directed by [Jacques Demy](/wiki/Jacques_Demy "Jacques Demy"), a story about Madame Emery and her 17-year-old daughter Geneviève ([Catherine Deneuve](/wiki/Catherine_Deneuve "Catherine Deneuve")) who sell umbrellas at their tiny boutique. The film was shot in the summer of 1963, and still contributes to the international renown of the city. Yet long before, at the time of the splendor of the transatlantic liners, Cherbourg was a port of arrival, departure or transit for many stars, including [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") and [Burt Lancaster](/wiki/Burt_Lancaster "Burt Lancaster"). The city was also birthplace of the filmmaker [Jean-Charles Tacchella](/wiki/Jean-Charles_Tacchella "Jean-Charles Tacchella") and the actor [Jean Marais](/wiki/Jean_Marais "Jean Marais").

[Festival of cinemas of Ireland and Great Britain](/w/index.php?title=Festival_des_cin%C3%A9mas_d%27Irlande_et_de_Grande-Bretagne_de_Cherbourg-Octeville&action=edit&redlink=1 "Festival des cinémas d'Irlande et de Grande-Bretagne de Cherbourg-Octeville (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Festival_des_cin%C3%A9mas_d%27Irlande_et_de_Grande-Bretagne_de_Cherbourg-Octeville "fr:Festival des cinémas d'Irlande et de Grande-Bretagne de Cherbourg-Octeville")], the [Cinemovida](/w/index.php?title=Cinemovida&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cinemovida (page does not exist)") (festival of the cinema of Spain and Latin America), and Images d'Outre-Rhin (German cinema), as well as Cin'étoiles, screenings of films outdoors in July, animate the local cultural life.

In 2003, the [EICAR](/wiki/%C3%89cole_Internationale_de_Cr%C3%A9ation_Audiovisuelle_et_de_R%C3%A9alisation "École Internationale de Création Audiovisuelle et de Réalisation") film school was located in the old buildings of the [marine hospital](/w/index.php?title=H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas "fr:Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas")]. After three years of loss and liabilities estimated at €1.5 million, it was placed into liquidation in September 2006 and replaced the following month, under the leadership of elders of its teachers by the Institut des métiers du cinéma de Normandie (IMC Normandie) that itself closed its doors in 2011.

The city has a fleet of 17 permanent cinema rooms, distributed over two sites, including one labelled as *Art et essai* ([Revival house](/wiki/Revival_house "Revival house")).

* ***Odéon*** (5 rooms)

Following the opening of the [CGR](/w/index.php?title=Circuit_Georges-Raymond&action=edit&redlink=1 "Circuit Georges-Raymond (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circuit_Georges-Raymond "fr:Circuit Georges-Raymond")] [multiplex](/wiki/Multiplex_(movie_theater) "Multiplex (movie theater)"), the withdrawal of the [Soredic](/w/index.php?title=Soredic&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soredic (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soredic "fr:Soredic")], which operated *Club 6* (*Rue de la Paix*) since 1983 and the *Odeon* (*Rue Foch*) since 1991, resulted in the closure in 2004 of the first and the revival of the latter city centre cinema, labelled as *[Art et essai](/wiki/Revival_house "Revival house")* by Fadila Chambelland, the former manager. The cinema had 90,000 admissions in 2006.

The damaged façade of the old cafe of the Grand balcon, which then became *Le Central* cinema, is in the style of the Second Empire, with [caryatids](/wiki/Caryatid "Caryatid") and garlands of flowers.

* ***Méga CGR*** (12 rooms)

Opened in 2003 near the *boulevard maritime* with 2,557 seats; 400,000 admissions in 2006.

* ***Omnia*** (1 room)

The historic cinema operated by [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé"), located on the *Rue de la Paix*, it was bought in the 1990s by the municipality and no longer welcomes any more than rare events. The interior frescoes of R. Lecoq, representing [Aeolus](/wiki/Aeolus "Aeolus") and [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan_(mythology) "Vulcan (mythology)"), were distinguished in 2006 by the label "20th century heritage" of the [Ministry of Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_(France) "Ministry of Culture (France)").[[145]](#cite_note-146)

Several cinemas have disappeared, such as the *Eldorado* (destroyed, *Place de la Republique*), the *Eden* (*Rue Cachin*), the *Vox* (former [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") room which became a second room of [Le Trident (theatre)](/wiki/Le_Trident_(theatre) "Le Trident (theatre)")), and the *Saint-Joseph* (*Rue des Ormes*), etc.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Language")]

The Cherbourg population spoke *le haguais*, a variant of [Cotentinais](/wiki/Cotentinais "Cotentinais") [Norman](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language"), while having some specifics regarding the pronunciation of certain words.

In Cotentinais Norman, Cherbourg is called *Tchidbouo* [[tʃidbwu:]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") and Octeville, *Otteville* [[ɔtvil]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"). Their inhabitants are the *Tchidbouorqŭais* and the *Ottevillais* [[tʃidbwuʁtʃje:]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") and [[ɔtvile:]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French").

While French was necessary in [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen") in the 19th century, Norman remained widely used from Cherbourg to [Caen](/wiki/Caen "Caen"), up to the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").[[146]](#cite_note-pic-147)

[Alfred Rossel](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Rossel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alfred Rossel (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfred_Rossel "fr:Alfred Rossel")] was the leading local figure of the dialect authors of the 19th century. He published his *Chansonnettes normandes*, among which *Sus la mé* became an anthem of the [Cotentin Peninsula](/wiki/Cotentin_Peninsula "Cotentin Peninsula"). At that time, [Jean Fleury](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Fleury_(writer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean Fleury (writer) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Fleury "fr:Jean Fleury")] was critical of its approximate spelling and poor mastery of the language.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

Various actors are now trying to promote the local use of the Norman. The Alfred Rossel society makes live folklore and language. *Le Boué-jaun* a Cherbourg-based magazine, published his texts in Norman, and one of three popular Norman universities is based here.[[146]](#cite_note-pic-147)

### Gastronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Gastronomy")]

A large fishing port, Cherbourg-Octeville offers a wide variety of fish ([yellowtail](/wiki/Yellowtail_(fish) "Yellowtail (fish)"), [bar](/wiki/European_seabass "European seabass"), [plaice](/wiki/Plaice "Plaice"), [mackerel](/wiki/Mackerel "Mackerel"), [rays](/wiki/Batoidea "Batoidea"), [red mullet](/wiki/Red_mullet "Red mullet"), [pollock](/wiki/Pollock "Pollock"), [lemon sole](/wiki/Lemon_sole "Lemon sole"), [small-spotted catshark](/wiki/Small-spotted_catshark "Small-spotted catshark"), etc.), crustaceans ([brown crab](/wiki/Cancer_pagurus "Cancer pagurus"), [spider crab](/wiki/Majoidea "Majoidea"), [lobster](/wiki/Homarus "Homarus")) and shellfish ([Saint-Jacques](/wiki/Pecten_maximus "Pecten maximus"), [scallops](/wiki/Scallop "Scallop"), [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel")), caught off the coast of the Cotentin peninsula.[[148]](#cite_note-149) The so-called [Demoiselles de Cherbourg](/w/index.php?title=Demoiselles_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Demoiselles de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demoiselles_de_Cherbourg "fr:Demoiselles de Cherbourg")] are small lobsters. Cherbourg is also located near three [oyster](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster") areas ([Blainville](/wiki/Blainville-sur-Mer "Blainville-sur-Mer"), [Saint-Vaast](/wiki/Saint-Vaast-la-Hougue "Saint-Vaast-la-Hougue") and [Isigny](/wiki/Isigny-sur-Mer "Isigny-sur-Mer")). The most traditional preparation is the [matelote](/wiki/Matelote "Matelote").[[149]](#cite_note-viquet-150) [Alexandre Dumas](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas "Alexandre Dumas") also presented the recipe of the *"queue de merlan à la mode de Cherbourg "* [tail of whiting in the Cherbourg manner], with butter and oysters.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

From 1464, the bakers of Cherbourg held Royal permission to develop their breads based on seawater, thus avoiding paying for the salt and the *gabelle* [salt tax]. On the occasion of the visit of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), they would have created folded bread, country bread ball, oval, which is folded back on itself to be cooked, thus offering a tighter bicorn-shaped sandwich which came to be called *"pain Napoléon"* [Napoleon bread].[[149]](#cite_note-viquet-150) Fleury indicated that at the beginning of the 19th century, the principal food of Nord-Cotentin was [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") bread, [buckwheat](/wiki/Buckwheat "Buckwheat") porridge and pork-based products, and on feast days, the *galette*, a "type of dough composed of buckwheat flour, milk and eggs, and cooked in a thin film on the tile with butter", watered, of course, with cider.[[55]](#cite_note-fleury1839-notions-56)

The agglomeration is located in the [AOC](/wiki/Appellation_d%27origine_contr%C3%B4l%C3%A9e "Appellation d'origine contrôlée") areas of the [Pont-l'Évêque](/wiki/Pont-l%27%C3%89v%C3%AAque_cheese "Pont-l'Évêque cheese") and of the [Camembert of Normandy](/w/index.php?title=Camembert_of_Normandy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Camembert of Normandy (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Camembert_de_Normandie "fr:Camembert de Normandie")] as well as being partially within the [Calvados](/wiki/Calvados "Calvados"), [Pommeau de Normandie](/wiki/Pommeau_de_Normandie "Pommeau de Normandie") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pommeau_de_Normandie "fr:Pommeau de Normandie")] and the [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider") of Normandy. It also benefits from the [IGP](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union") of the cider of Normandy, Normandy pork and [Normandy poultry](/w/index.php?title=Volailles_de_Normandie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Volailles de Normandie (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volailles_de_Normandie "fr:Volailles de Normandie")].[[151]](#cite_note-152) More broadly, the kitchen of Nord-Cotentin is [that of Normandy](/w/index.php?title=Cuisine_normande&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cuisine normande (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_normande "fr:Cuisine normande")], in which dairy products (butter, cream, milk, cheese, etc.) and apples (as fruit or alcohol) dominate.

Since 2010, the restaurant *le Pily*, of the [Valognes](/wiki/Valognes "Valognes") restaurateur Pierre Marion, holds a star in the [Michelin Guide](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide").[[152]](#cite_note-153)

### Heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Heritage")]

#### Civil monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Civil monuments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Cherbourg_theatre.jpg/250px-Cherbourg_theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg_theatre.jpg)

The Italian theatre.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Cherbourg-Gare-transatlantique-pano.jpg/250px-Cherbourg-Gare-transatlantique-pano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg-Gare-transatlantique-pano.jpg)

The *gare maritime*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Cherbourg_Bassin_du_Commerce.jpg/250px-Cherbourg_Bassin_du_Commerce.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg_Bassin_du_Commerce.jpg)

The commercial dock, during the [Tall Ships' Race 2005](/wiki/Tall_Ships%27_Races "Tall Ships' Races").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Hotel_Atlantique_Cherbourg2.jpg/250px-Hotel_Atlantique_Cherbourg2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotel_Atlantique_Cherbourg2.jpg)

The central pavilion of the Atlantic Hotel.

The [Italian Theatre](/wiki/Le_Trident_(theatre) "Le Trident (theatre)") is one of the last [Italian theatres](/wiki/Theater_(building) "Theater (building)") built in France (1880). Opened in 1882, it was built on the plans of [Charles de Lalande](/w/index.php?title=Charles_de_Lalande&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles de Lalande (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_de_Lalande "fr:Charles de Lalande")], on the site of the [grain market](/wiki/Grain_trade "Grain trade"). The façade pays homage to [Molière](/wiki/Moli%C3%A8re "Molière"), [Boieldieu](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Adrien_Boieldieu "François-Adrien Boieldieu") and [Corneille](/wiki/Pierre_Corneille "Pierre Corneille"). It has been classified a historical monument since 1984 with its two side returns and corresponding roofing; also classified are the vestibule, the grand staircase, the hall and foyer, as well as the 13 original decorations. The ceiling is the work of [Georges Clairin](/wiki/Georges_Clairin "Georges Clairin"). With three galleries, it accommodates up to 600 spectators.

The Mouchel Fountain, named after the patron and director of the journal *Le Phare de la Manche*, stands at the centre of the *Place Général-de-Gaulle*. A monumental fountain in cast iron, it was created by [Louis Eugène Gutelle](/w/index.php?title=Louis_Eug%C3%A8ne_Gutelle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis Eugène Gutelle (page does not exist)") in 1895.

The Hotel Epron de la Horie (named after the Vice Admiral and Marine Minister [Jacques Epron de la Horie](/w/index.php?title=Louis-Jacques_Epron_de_la_Horie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis-Jacques Epron de la Horie (page does not exist)"), owner under the first Empire) or ancient customs is located at the corner of *Rue de Val-de-Saire* and the wharf of the Old Arsenal. Built in 1781 by Jacques Martin Maurice, "contractor of the King's works" in [schist](/wiki/Schist "Schist") (cover and body of the building) and red brick (window frames), registered as a historical monument since 16 February 1965.[[153]](#cite_note-154) Successively barracks of the Swiss, auxiliary Hospital of the work of the harbour, home of shipowners Richer, Cousin, Despréaux and Lias in the 19th century and a customs house during the interwar period, it is now the headquarters of the [Groupe Caisse d'Épargne](/wiki/Groupe_Caisse_d%27%C3%89pargne "Groupe Caisse d'Épargne").[[56]](#cite_note-centreurbainancien-57)

The former [Gare Maritime de Cherbourg](/wiki/Gare_Maritime_de_Cherbourg "Gare Maritime de Cherbourg") is the largest French [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") monument. Built by [René Levavasseur](/w/index.php?title=Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architect)&action=edit&redlink=1 "René Levavasseur (architect) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architecte) "fr:René Levavasseur (architecte)")] from 1928 and inaugurated in 1933 by [President Lebrun](/wiki/Albert_Fran%C3%A7ois_Lebrun "Albert François Lebrun"), it could accommodate two ships simultaneously. Listed as an [historical monument](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") in 1989 and 2000, it was redeveloped in 2002 to become an oceanographic complex, the [Cité de la Mer](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_de_la_Mer "Cité de la Mer"), where one can visit the [SNLE](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") [*Le Redoutable*](/wiki/Redoutable-class_submarine_(1967) "Redoutable-class submarine (1967)"), and host since December 2006 of a cruise terminal.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

The *hôtel Atlantique* [Atlantic Hotel], opposite the maritime station, was also built by [René Levavasseur](/w/index.php?title=Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architect)&action=edit&redlink=1 "René Levavasseur (architect) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Levavasseur_(architecte) "fr:René Levavasseur (architecte)")], in iron and cement and in the [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") style, for the three transatlantic companies which served Cherbourg, the [Cunard Line](/wiki/Cunard_Line "Cunard Line"), the [White Star Line](/wiki/White_Star_Line "White Star Line") and the [Red Star Line](/wiki/Red_Star_Line "Red Star Line"), grouped into the *Société anonyme de l’Hôtel Atlantique*. It hosted on 5,400 m2 (58,000 sq ft) the emigrants (third class passengers), mainly from Eastern Europe, who stayed there for an average of 12 days to undergo sanitary and customs controls. The building thus included a section for infected and a section for disinfected with a capacity of 2,000 people. Begun in 1920, opened in 1926, it closed eight years later. Requisitioned under the Occupation and Liberation, it was bought by [Félix Amiot](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Amiot "Félix Amiot") to accommodate some of its shipyard employees.[[155]](#cite_note-156) It has hosted the services of the [Chamber of commerce and industry of Cherbourg-Cotentin](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_of_commerce_and_industry_of_Cherbourg-Cotentin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chamber of commerce and industry of Cherbourg-Cotentin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chambre_de_commerce_et_d%27industrie_de_Cherbourg-Cotentin "fr:Chambre de commerce et d'industrie de Cherbourg-Cotentin")] since 1991. The central pavilion is included in the inventory of historic monuments since October 2001.[[10]](#cite_note-pedestre-11)

Statues of Themis and Minerva, Roman goddesses of justice and war respectively, of [Houdon](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_Houdon "Jean-Antoine Houdon") and [Roland](/wiki/Philippe-Laurent_Roland "Philippe-Laurent Roland") and which were stored in the courtyard of the [Palais Bourbon](/wiki/Palais_Bourbon "Palais Bourbon") during their replacement on the frontispiece of the Chamber of Deputies by casts during the renovation of the façade, were available to the city in June 1989, through [Olivier Stirn](/w/index.php?title=Olivier_Stirn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Olivier Stirn (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olivier_Stirn "fr:Olivier Stirn")], Minister of Tourism, and President of the *Communauté urbaine de Cherbourg* [Urban community of Cherbourg]. After restoration by Pierre Bataille of [Poclain](/wiki/Poclain "Poclain"), they were each placed in 1990 and 1993 on a roundabout, the *Minerva* of Philippe-Laurent Roland, near the [Cité de la Mer](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_de_la_Mer "Cité de la Mer"), the *Themis* of Jean-Antoine Houdon at the foot of the Montagne du Roule. Carved around 1810, they have been classified as historic monuments since June 1990.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

The town hall was built at the beginning of the 19th century; It was enlarged twice, first in 1850 by a south-west wing forming an L-shape with the first building, and then under the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") (salon of the Empress), and reworked after the Liberation. Inside, a staircase serves the Grand Lounge and the lounge of the Empress, which houses portraits of Napoleon III and Eugénie by [Winterhalter](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_Winterhalter "Franz Xaver Winterhalter"), with - between the two – a rotunda room for the paintings of Michel-Adrien Servant recalling the major events in the history of the city. Since 1858, the Council Chamber contains the 16th century chimney of the abbot's house of the [Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu](/w/index.php?title=Abbey_of_Notre-Dame-du-V%C5%93u&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu (page does not exist)"), purchased by the municipality in 1841 and classified as a historical monument since 1905.[[56]](#cite_note-centreurbainancien-57) The three lounges and the stairs have been registered since 13 August 2004.

The [maritime hospital](/w/index.php?title=H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B4pital_des_arm%C3%A9es_Ren%C3%A9-Le-Bas "fr:Hôpital des armées René-Le-Bas")], a former regional hospital of the armies of René-Le-Bas, built by a decision of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") and opened 15 February 1869, it was decommissioned in 2000 and rehabilitated as an academic and cultural centre in 2002. The [Napoleon III style](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture "Second Empire architecture") buildings are surrounded by a large park.

The docks and Port Chantereyne are regularly brought to life by various events: Stopovers of prestigious liners (*[Queen Elizabeth 2](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_2 "Queen Elizabeth 2")*, [*Queen Mary 2*](/wiki/RMS_Queen_Mary_2 "RMS Queen Mary 2"), etc.), armada, sailing races and such. The docks were constructed in 1994 with the lighting by [Yann Kersalé](/wiki/Yann_Kersal%C3%A9 "Yann Kersalé"). The marina, first French port of call, extends beyond the *Plage Verte*, old beach redeveloped into lawn after the creation of the port. Recreational and leisure facilities are located here (pool, skating rink, bowling, services for boaters, etc.). In the commercial wet dock is the *Jacques-Louise*, the last wooden trawler built in the shipyards of Cherbourg Bellot in 1959, the former Cherbourg [Blue Riband](/wiki/Blue_Riband "Blue Riband"), decommissioned in 1991, registered in 1996, then classified as a historic monument in 1999. A trawler in oak wood of the Orne, designed for lateral fishing off the coast, it has been open to the public since summer 2004.[[157]](#cite_note-158)

#### Memorials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Memorials")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Statue_%C3%A9questre_de_Napol%C3%A9on.jpg/250px-Statue_%C3%A9questre_de_Napol%C3%A9on.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_%C3%A9questre_de_Napol%C3%A9on.jpg)

Statue of Napoleon

The [Equestrian Statue of Napoleon I](/w/index.php?title=Place_Napol%C3%A9on_(Cherbourg-Octeville)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Place Napoléon (Cherbourg-Octeville) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Place_Napol%C3%A9on_(Cherbourg-Octeville) "fr:Place Napoléon (Cherbourg-Octeville)")] faces the basilica, on the *Place Napoléon*. The work of [Armand Le Véel](/wiki/Armand_Le_V%C3%A9el "Armand Le Véel"), it represents the Emperor contemplating the [harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour") and the [military port](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base"). On the base, reads an excerpt from the *Memorial of Sainte-Hélène*, dated 15 July 1816: *I had resolved to renew to Cherbourg the wonders of Egypt*, i.e. a pyramid with central fort and a new [Lake Moeris](/wiki/Lake_Moeris "Lake Moeris"), for the outer harbour, dug into the rock. The statue, erected in 1858 on the occasion of the visit of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III"), recalls the importance of the Emperor in the expansion of Cherbourg. Around this emblematic monument of the city, registered in August 2006 and classified as an [historic monument](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") on 31 January 2008, extends the *Plage verte*, the old artificial beach until the postwar period, which runs along the marina.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

The monument of the [Duke of Berry](/wiki/Charles_Ferdinand,_Duke_of_Berry "Charles Ferdinand, Duke of Berry"), in the *Place de la République*, commemorates the landing of the son of the future [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France"), back in France on the British frigate *Eurotas* on 13 April 1814, after the fall of the Empire. Completed in 1816, it consists of an obelisk of 25 ft (7.6 m) in pink granite of Flamanville, surmounting a fountain of grey granite, where four bronze lions' heads spew water into a basin dug in the same block.[[56]](#cite_note-centreurbainancien-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/ArmandDeBricqueville.jpg/250px-ArmandDeBricqueville.jpg)](/wiki/File:ArmandDeBricqueville.jpg)

Bricqueville by David d’Angers.

The bust of [Colonel Bricqueville](/w/index.php?title=Armand_de_Bricqueville&action=edit&redlink=1 "Armand de Bricqueville (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armand_de_Bricqueville "fr:Armand de Bricqueville")], on the *Quai de Caligny*, was inaugurated on 12 May 1850, in homage to the Colonel of the Imperial Dragons and Bonapartist deputy of Cherbourg who died in 1844. This 1.45 m (4.8 ft) bust of Hermes[[159]](#cite_note-160) is a bronze of [David d'Angers](/wiki/David_d%27Angers "David d'Angers") on a 4 ft (1.2 m) column of granite of the architect Lemelle, on which one can read the name of four battles where Bricqueville is illustrated: [Wagram](/wiki/Battle_of_Wagram "Battle of Wagram"), [Krasnoi](/wiki/Battle_of_Krasnoi "Battle of Krasnoi"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Siege_of_Antwerp_(1814) "Siege of Antwerp (1814)") and [Versailles](/wiki/Battle_of_Rocquencourt "Battle of Rocquencourt"). Two bronze reliefs evoke the military (a sword) and parliamentary (a forum), were melted by the Germans in 1944. The monument has been listed as an [historical monument](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") since August 2006.[[160]](#cite_note-statuaire-161)

The statue of [Jean-François Millet](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Millet "Jean-François Millet"), inaugurated in the [public garden](/w/index.php?title=Public_garden_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Public garden of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardin_public_de_Cherbourg "fr:Jardin public de Cherbourg")] on 22 September 1892, for the centenary of the [First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), honours the "painter of peasants", student at the Museum of Cherbourg. Funded by a subscription launched by the municipality in 1886 and taken over by Parisian circles, the realisation of the [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") bust (1.05 m (3.4 ft) high) was entrusted to [Henri Chapu](/wiki/Henri_Chapu "Henri Chapu"); after his death, it was completed by his pupil [Jean-Ernest Bouteiller](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Ernest_Bouteiller&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean-Ernest Bouteiller (page does not exist)") who had assisted him in the allegorical group in bronze (2.95 m (9.7 ft) high) of a peasant woman with her daughter in arms and laying flowers of the fields on the bust, supported on a pedestal and granite rocks (4.45 m (14.6 ft) high, 2.55 m (8.4 ft) wide, 2.6 m (8.5 ft) of depth). The monument is inscribed since August 2006.[[160]](#cite_note-statuaire-161)

The monument to the dead of the [*Surcouf*](/wiki/French_submarine_Surcouf "French submarine Surcouf"), inaugurated at the end of the marina pier on 23 September 1951 by General de Gaulle, commemorates the memory of 130 sailors from the [Free French Naval Forces](/wiki/Free_French_Naval_Forces "Free French Naval Forces") submarine, built at Cherbourg and which sank on 18 February 1942 in the Pacific.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

#### Military monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Military monuments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Cherbourg-Fort-de-l%27ouest.jpg/250px-Cherbourg-Fort-de-l%27ouest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherbourg-Fort-de-l%27ouest.jpg)

Fort de l’Ouest.

[Cherbourg Harbour](/wiki/Cherbourg_Harbour "Cherbourg Harbour") is the largest artificial harbour of the world. Begun in 1783, the central wall was completed in 1853 and equipped with three forts in 1860. Built 4 km (2.5 mi) from the coast, the offshore seawall is 3.64 km (2.26 mi) long, with an average width of 100 m (330 ft) at its base and 12 m (39 ft) at its peak, and a height of 27 m (89 ft). The three sea walls cover over 6 km (3.7 mi) combined.

The fort de l’Île Pelée [fort of Pelée Island], a defensive element to the east of the sea wall, was designed by Ricard and Decaux and built between 1777 and 1784. It was named *fort Royal, fort National, fort Imperial*, before taking the name of the island on which it was built. It served as a prison during the Revolution.

Fort du Roule (Museum of the War and Liberation) is located on the *Montagne du Roule*. The location in 1650 of the Chapel of Notre-Dame-de-Protection, abandoned during the Revolution, razed in 1870, this highest point of the city [117 m (384 ft)] welcomed a redoubt to protect the harbour in 1793. In 1853, the present fort was built. The place of the last fighting in 1940, it was reinforced by the Germans in 1943 with a battery located on the hillside overlooking the harbour, below the fort. Composed of four [casemates](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate") for 105 mm (4.1 in) guns and a position for the direction of firing, with several tunnels and access dug into the rock, for the Germans it became the strong point of the *fortresse de Cherbourg* and of the [Atlantic Wall](/wiki/Atlantic_Wall "Atlantic Wall"). On 6 June 1954, [René Coty](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Coty "René Coty") inaugurated the first French Museum of Liberation there. At the end of a winding road named *chemin des Résistants* [Path of Resistants], the fort offers a panoramic view of the city and the harbour. The battery and a part of the German ammunition storage tunnels were classified as a historic monument in 1995, and another part is converted into an underground laboratory for measurement of radioactivity for the school of military application of atomic energy.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

#### Religious monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Religious monuments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/FranceNormandieCherbourgBasiliqueTrinite.jpg/250px-FranceNormandieCherbourgBasiliqueTrinite.jpg)](/wiki/File:FranceNormandieCherbourgBasiliqueTrinite.jpg)

The [Basilica of Sainte-Trinité](/w/index.php?title=Basilica_of_Sainte-Trinit%C3%A9_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Basilica of Sainte-Trinité of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basilique_Sainte-Trinit%C3%A9_de_Cherbourg "fr:Basilique Sainte-Trinité de Cherbourg")].

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Eglise_ND_du_Voeu_Cherbourg1.jpg/250px-Eglise_ND_du_Voeu_Cherbourg1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eglise_ND_du_Voeu_Cherbourg1.jpg)

The Church of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu.

The [Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu](/w/index.php?title=Abbey_of_Notre-Dame-du-V%C5%93u&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abbaye_Notre-Dame_du_V%C5%93u "fr:Abbaye Notre-Dame du Vœu")] was founded in 1145, on the coast of [Équeurdreville](/wiki/%C3%89queurdreville-Hainneville "Équeurdreville-Hainneville"), at the *Croûte du Homet*, by [Empress Matilda](/wiki/Empress_Matilda "Empress Matilda"). Located outside the city walls, it was regularly looted and burned during the incessant Anglo-French battles, then during the [Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"). Subject to the regime of a [commendatory abbot](/wiki/Commendatory_abbot "Commendatory abbot") in 1583, it declined until its closure in 1774. Its lands were annexed in 1778 for the construction of the [military port](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base"), and it became the residence of the [Duke of Harcourt](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Henri_d%27Harcourt "François-Henri d'Harcourt"), who sheltered the King in 1786. The place was then transformed into a hospital, into a [prison](/w/index.php?title=Bagne_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bagne de Cherbourg (page does not exist)"), and into the Martin des Pallières Barracks for the [marine infantry](/wiki/Marines "Marines").

The [company town](/wiki/Company_town "Company town") of Chantereyne was built in 1928, until its destruction in June 1944. Bought by the Town Hall in 1961, the Abbey has been slowly restored since 1965. The smokestack of the Abbey House (16th century) is kept in the council room of the city hall, the west portal of the Church (13th century) is placed in the [public garden](/w/index.php?title=Jardin_public_de_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jardin public de Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardin_public_de_Cherbourg "fr:Jardin public de Cherbourg")]. The remains of Martin des Pallières barracks were classified in 1913, then all of the buildings, remains and soil of the abbey, in September 2002. The grave slab of Guillaume de Margerai, priest of [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville"), who died in the 1280s, uncovered, has been classified as an [historic monument](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") since 1995.

The [Basilica of Sainte-Trinité](/w/index.php?title=Basilica_of_Sainte-Trinit%C3%A9_of_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Basilica of Sainte-Trinité of Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basilique_Sainte-Trinit%C3%A9_de_Cherbourg "fr:Basilique Sainte-Trinité de Cherbourg")], begun in the 11th century at the request of [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror"), remained the only parish church of the city until the 19th century. The stately church dedicated to [Our Lady](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)") in the castle was destroyed along with the fortress, in the 17th century. The Trinité was enlarged and transformed significantly in the 13th century, the [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") was rebuilt, the [choir](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)") and the bell tower is recorded from after 1450. After a rampage by January 1794, it had added a new square bell tower 26 m (85 ft) in 1828 and restored in neo-[flamboyant](/wiki/Gothic_architecture#Flamboyant_arch "Gothic architecture") style in 1865. Registered as a historical monument since March 1944, Trinité has a rich religious furniture, including a high [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") of 1809, a wood [pulpit](/wiki/Pulpit "Pulpit") carved by [Pierre Fréret](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Fr%C3%A9ret&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Fréret (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_Fr%C3%A9ret "fr:Pierre Fréret")] (1767), a [retable](/wiki/Retable "Retable") of [Armand Fréret](/w/index.php?title=Armand_Fr%C3%A9ret&action=edit&redlink=1 "Armand Fréret (page does not exist)") (1814) and the great [organs](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ") by [Cavaillé-Coll](/wiki/Aristide_Cavaill%C3%A9-Coll "Aristide Cavaillé-Coll").

The church of Notre-Dame du Roule was built at the foot of the *Montagne du Roule* between 1832 and 1842 under the leadership of the "poet-Barber" Michel Legoupil and by the subscription of the faithful of the peripheral quarters of Roule which grew, such as the districts of the Vœu and the Polle.

The church of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu, begun in 1850 on subscription of the parishioners and in the [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") style due to the scarcity of resources, was erected on a pasture, known as *les briques*, offered by Mr. de Virandeville. In 1855, the municipality completes the nave inaugurated in 1852 by a [transept](/wiki/Transept "Transept") and a more ornate choir, and in 1862 the façade and two bell towers. A work of 61.5 m (202 ft) in length, the church houses a large organ by Duputel (1885), classified as a historic monument since 1990 and the stained glass windows of 1834, 1858–60 and 1949–58.[[163]](#cite_note-jeanxxii-164)

The Church of Saint-Clément was built within the quarter of the Val-de-Saire, facing the Pasteur hospital, between June 1853 and 1856 by the architect of the city, Geufroy. 52 m (171 ft) long, it is of Greco-Roman inspiration, with a porch in the triangular pediment supported by four columns with [Doric](/wiki/Doric_order "Doric order") [capitals](/wiki/Capital_(architecture) "Capital (architecture)"). It houses the altars of the Virgin (1863) by François Fréret and Saint-Clément (1864) by Louis-Victor Fréret, acquired from the Basilica of Sainte-Trinité in 1846, an organ (1881), painting of the [twelve apostles](/wiki/Twelve_apostles "Twelve apostles") (1935) the Rock of Césigné and stained glass (1953) of Mauméjean.[[164]](#cite_note-165)

The Church of Saint-Pierre and Saint-Paul, on the area of Octeville, was built between 1967 and 1969 while the "grand ensemble" of Provinces was born. The triangular and irregular modern architecture of [Paul Vimond](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Vimond&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paul Vimond (page does not exist)") symbolises "the tent of God in among the houses of men", a sacred art inspired by the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") (1962-1965).[[165]](#cite_note-166) Another Church was built on Octeville during those years: The Church of Sainte-Marie-Madeleine-Postel opened in 1966 in the quarter of Fourches and decommissioned in 1990.

The Church of Saint-Martin of Octeville, dating from the 12th century, is the historic parish church of Octeville which depended on the [Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu](/w/index.php?title=Abbey_of_Notre-Dame-du-V%C5%93u&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abbey of Notre-Dame-du-Vœu (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abbaye_Notre-Dame_du_V%C5%93u "fr:Abbaye Notre-Dame du Vœu")]. Romanesque, it has an octagonal [saddleback steeple](/w/index.php?title=Saddleback_steeple&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saddleback steeple (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clocher_en_b%C3%A2ti%C3%A8re "fr:Clocher en bâtière")]. The nave was remodelled in the 18th century. A [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") depicting the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") has been classified as a historic monument since 1908.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

## Military life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Military life")]

Main article: [Cherbourg Naval Base](/wiki/Cherbourg_Naval_Base "Cherbourg Naval Base")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Abeille_Libert%C3%A9.jpg/250px-Abeille_Libert%C3%A9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abeille_Libert%C3%A9.jpg)

Arrival of the *[Abeille Liberté](/wiki/Abeille_Libert%C3%A9 "Abeille Liberté")* at Cherbourg-Octeville.

During the Middle Ages, Cherbourg, a stronghold of the Cotentin peninsula, was home to a small garrison for the protection of the fortress. With the implementation of the harbour and military port, Cherbourg became a port of war at the end of the 18th century, with a large garrison. In 1798, it had 1,332 men, or a tenth of the population, divided mainly between the barracks of the Abbey, current historical Service of the Navy, which housed 542 men of the 4th brigade and the Maurice Quarter, in the *Hôtel Epron de la Horie*, home to 227 men.[[167]](#cite_note-168) Numbers were brought to 3,000 men for the completion of the work, by a decree of germinal year XI.

During the 20th century, Cherbourg, a strategic point during both world wars, adapted to new threats. It then hosted a large garrison of the Navy, an artillery regiment and a *Hôpital des Armées* known locally as "marine hospital". In the late 1990s and early 2000s, the presence of the army weakened by the transfer of the northern fleet to Brest and the closure of the maritime hospital renamed René-Le-Bas.

Yet Cherbourg remained a base of the first order of the [National Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy"), as the seat of the [Maritime Prefecture of Manche and the North Sea](/w/index.php?title=Maritime_Prefecture_of_Manche_and_the_North_Sea&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maritime Prefecture of Manche and the North Sea (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pr%C3%A9fecture_maritime_de_la_Manche_et_la_mer_du_Nord "fr:Préfecture maritime de la Manche et la mer du Nord")] and of the Maritime Gendarmerie grouping of Manche. The naval base is the homeport of five patrol vessels of the Navy and the coastguard, group of the clearance divers sleeve and its building-base the [*Vulcain*](/wiki/Vulcain_(M611) "Vulcain (M611)"), the tug *[Abeille Liberté](/wiki/Abeille_Libert%C3%A9 "Abeille Liberté")* and various support vessels. It is also the headquarters of the Operational Training of Surveillance and Territorial Information of Cherbourg (Cherbourg FOSIT) which brings together thirteen semaphores and the lookout of the maritime district. In addition, a flotilla 35 F [Dauphin](/wiki/Eurocopter_AS365_Dauphin "Eurocopter AS365 Dauphin") helicopter is based at [Cherbourg – Maupertus Airport](/wiki/Cherbourg_%E2%80%93_Maupertus_Airport "Cherbourg – Maupertus Airport"). The operation of the military port is borne by the directions of the Commissioner of the Navy, maritime works and information systems of the Navy, as well as the branch of the support service of the fleet and the military workshop of the Cherbourg fleet.[[168]](#cite_note-accueil-169)

Cherbourg is also a training hub of the armed forces through the [School of Military Applications of Atomic Energy](/w/index.php?title=School_of_Military_Applications_of_Atomic_Energy&action=edit&redlink=1 "School of Military Applications of Atomic Energy (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_applications_militaires_de_l%27%C3%A9nergie_atomique "fr:École des applications militaires de l'énergie atomique")] (EAMEA), in charge of the joint education of military specialists in material sciences, of techniques and of nuclear safety and the *École des fourriers de [Querqueville](/wiki/Querqueville "Querqueville")*, devoted to education of the officers of the three armed forces in business administration, management, human resources and the restoration to the training of specialists of the restoration of the national gendarmerie and the homes of the Navy staff.

Proposals for reform on the organization and the distribution of the French Army, presented in the spring of 2008 in the [White Paper on Defence and National Security](/wiki/2008_French_White_Paper_on_Defence_and_National_Security "2008 French White Paper on Defence and National Security") planned in the context of the [French General Review of Public Policies](/wiki/French_General_Review_of_Public_Policies "French General Review of Public Policies") raise the concern of civilian personnel of the defence of the city, including the [construction of submarines](/w/index.php?title=List_of_submarines_built_at_Cherbourg&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of submarines built at Cherbourg (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liste_des_sous-marins_construits_%C3%A0_Cherbourg "fr:Liste des sous-marins construits à Cherbourg")]. According to projects, Cherbourg-Octeville would become one of 90 defence bases around 2010. In a pooling of means and the establishment of support for the armed forces, the city would retain military and civilian activities, and would host new regiments for the army and of the air force to constitute one of the largest bases of defence.[[169]](#cite_note-170) However, the Navy in Cherbourg-Octeville would lose 220 jobs, including civilians, through including the division of half of the staff of the Directorate of maritime works, the abolition of 30 posts including 5 civilians in the *École des fourriers* and the Atomic school, the loss of 27 posts including 14 civilians in the direction of information systems, and the disarmament of the *Vulcain*, *Acharné*, *Coralline* and *Élan*. National orders for [DCNS](/wiki/DCNS_(company) "DCNS (company)") could be spread over several years, also reducing human needs there, especially among the subcontractors.[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Several military units were stationed at Cherbourg during the 20th century:

* [8th Infantry Regiment](/w/index.php?title=8th_Infantry_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "8th Infantry Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/8e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27infanterie "fr:8e régiment d'infanterie")], 1939 - 1940
* [25th Infantry Regiment](/w/index.php?title=25th_Infantry_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "25th Infantry Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/25e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27infanterie "fr:25e régiment d'infanterie")], 1870 - 1914
* [119th Infantry Regiment](/w/index.php?title=119th_Infantry_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "119th Infantry Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/119e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27infanterie "fr:119e régiment d'infanterie")],
* [77th Territorial Infantry Regiment](/w/index.php?title=77th_Territorial_Infantry_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "77th Territorial Infantry Regiment (France) (page does not exist)"), 1870 - 1914
* [2nd Artillery Regiment of Foot](/w/index.php?title=2nd_Artillery_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "2nd Artillery Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/2e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27artillerie_(France) "fr:2e régiment d'artillerie (France)")], 1914
* [1st Marine Artillery Regiment](/wiki/1st_Marine_Artillery_Regiment "1st Marine Artillery Regiment"), 1906
* [2nd Colonial Artillery Regiment](/w/index.php?title=2nd_Colonial_Artillery_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "2nd Colonial Artillery Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/2e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27artillerie_coloniale "fr:2e régiment d'artillerie coloniale")], 1906
* [5th Colonial Artillery Regiment](/w/index.php?title=5th_Colonial_Artillery_Regiment_(France)&action=edit&redlink=1 "5th Colonial Artillery Regiment (France) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/5e_r%C3%A9giment_d%27artillerie_coloniale "fr:5e régiment d'artillerie coloniale")], 1906

## Gallery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Gallery")]

* [![Town centre](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/CherbourgCentre.JPG/120px-CherbourgCentre.JPG)](/wiki/File:CherbourgCentre.JPG "Town centre")

  Town centre
* [![Charles de Gaulle delivering a speech in liberated Cherbourg from the Hôtel de Ville (town hall)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/HD-SN-99-02715.JPEG/120px-HD-SN-99-02715.JPEG)](/wiki/File:HD-SN-99-02715.JPEG "Charles de Gaulle delivering a speech in liberated Cherbourg from the Hôtel de Ville (town hall)")

  [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") delivering a speech in liberated Cherbourg from the Hôtel de Ville (town hall)

## Bibliography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cherbourg&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Bibliography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Chbg-Voisinlahougue.png/250px-Chbg-Voisinlahougue.png)](/wiki/File:Chbg-Voisinlahougue.png)

*History of the city of Cherbourg*, by Voisin La Hougue, continued from 1728 until 1835 by Vérusmor (1835)

Published in the 19th century

* *Mémoires de la Société nationale académique de Cherbourg* [*Memoirs of the national academic society of Cherbourg*] (in French), 1833-1995
* de Berruyer (1833). *Guide du voyageur à Cherbourg* [*A traveller's guide to Cherbourg*] (in French). Cherbourg: Boulanger.
* Voisin La Hougue (1835). *Histoire de la ville de Cherbourg (continuée depuis 1728 jusqu'à 1835 par Vérusmor)* [*History of the city of Cherbourg (continued from 1728 until 1835 by Vérusmor)*] (in French). Cherbourg: Boulanger.
* de Tocqueville, Alexis (1848). *Notice sur Cherbourg* [*Notice on Cherbourg*] (in French). Paris: Furne et Cie.
* ["Cherbourg"](https://archive.org/stream/handbookfortrave1861john#page/82/mode/2up), *A Handbook for Travellers in France*, London: John Murray, 1861, [OL](/wiki/OL_(identifier) "OL (identifier)") [24627024M](https://openlibrary.org/books/OL24627024M)
* Pelloquet, Th. (1866). *Cherbourg et ses bains de mer* [*Cherbourg and its sea baths*] (in French). mprimerie Vallée, Paris.
* Liais, Eugène (1871). *Cherbourg, la ville, son port et son commerce* [*Cherbourg, the city, its port and its trade*] (in French). Mouchel.
* C.B. Black (1876), ["Cherbourg"](https://archive.org/stream/guidetonorthfra01blacgoog#page/n544/mode/2up), *Guide to the North of France*, Edinburgh: Adam and Charles Black
* Bertin, Émile (1879). *Fondation de l'ancien Port de Cherbourg (notes et plans)* [*Foundation of the old Port of Cherbourg (notes and plans)*] (in French). Paris: Dunod.
* Leroy, Abbé (1885). *Le Vieux Cherbourg* [*The old Cherbourg*] (in French). Mouchel.
